question: As per the Open Vial Policy, which  of the following multi-dose vaccine vials can be used over more than one  immunization session?  1. BCG Vaccine  2. DPT Vaccine  3. Hepatitis B Vaccine  4. Measles Vaccine Select the correct answer:
explanation:
Live attenuated
vaccines like BCG and Measles cannot be used for multiple sessions once
opened. Under the WHO Open Vial Policy, DPT, Hepatitis B, Oral Polio
Vaccine (OPV), liquid Pentavalent, Pneumococcal, and Injectable Polio
(IPV) can be used for more than one session (up to 28 days if kept in a
cold chain).

question: The statement "Health is a  dynamic equilibrium between man and his environment, and disease is a  maladjustment of the human organism to the environment" explains which  of the following concepts of health?

options.b: Explanation  & Tip:  • Biomedical: Focuses strictly on pathogens and genetics.  • Ecological: Focuses on the dynamic equilibrium between Man and  Environment.  • Psychological: Focuses on mental and emotional aspects.  • Holistic: Considers physical, mental, social, and spiritual  interconnections.  [Subject: PSM | Topic: National Health Programs & Policies]

explanation:
• Biomedical: Focuses strictly on pathogens and genetics.
• Ecological: Focuses on the dynamic equilibrium between Man and
Environment.
• Psychological: Focuses on mental and emotional aspects.
• Holistic: Considers physical, mental, social, and spiritual
interconnections.

question: Which of  the following correctly pairs the broad components of the National Health  Policy 2017 with their specific goals?  1. Health Status and Program Impact: Increase life expectancy at birth from  67.5 to 70 years.  2. Health System Performance: More than 90% of newborns fully  immunized by 1 year of age by 2025.    26    For More High-Yield Medical  Notes & Updates  Follow & DM: @itsgauravmalikk  3. Health System Strengthening: Increase State Sector Health spending to  more than 8% of their budget by 2020. Select the correct answer:

options.d: Explanation &  Tip: All three statements strictly align with the documented goals of the  National Health Policy 2017.  [Subject: PSM | Topic: Health Planning & Management]

explanation:
All three statements strictly align with the documented goals of the
National Health Policy 2017.

question: Which of the  following management methods/techniques in health services are based on  Behavioral Science?  1. System Analysis  2. Organizational Design  3. Personnel Management  4. Information Systems Select the correct answer:

options.b: Explanation & Tip:  Management techniques are divided into Quantitative and Behavioral.  System Analysis, Cost-Benefit, and Network Analysis are Quantitative.  Organizational Design, Personnel Management, Communication, and  Information Systems are Behavioral sciences.  [Subject: PSM | Topic: Health Planning & Management]

explanation:
Management techniques are divided into Quantitative and Behavioral.
System Analysis, Cost-Benefit, and Network Analysis are Quantitative.
Organizational Design, Personnel Management, Communication, and
Information Systems are Behavioral sciences.

question: In health  planning, the end results are qualified by specific characteristics. Consider the  following pairs:  1. Goal: Always constrained by time.  2. Objective: Precise, either achieved or not achieved.  3. Target: Permits the concept of degree of achievement. How many of the  above pairs are correctly matched?

options.b: Explanation & Tip:  Pair 1 is incorrect. A "Goal" is an ultimate desired state that is broad and  generally not constrained by specific time or resources. Objectives and  Targets are correct as stated.  [Subject: PSM | Topic: Health Planning & Management]

explanation:
Pair 1 is incorrect. A "Goal" is an ultimate desired state that is broad and
generally not constrained by specific time or resources. Objectives and
Targets are correct as stated.

question: As per the  planning cycle, what is the correct sequence of steps for a health program?

options.c: Explanation & Tip: Health planning always begins with identifying and  prioritizing the core problem, followed by selecting a strategy, executing it, and  finally evaluating the outcome.  [Subject: PSM | Topic: Epidemiology]
options.d: Selecting the best program $\rightarrow$ Prioritizing the  problem $\rightarrow$ Evaluation $\rightarrow$ Operating

explanation:
Health planning always begins with identifying and
prioritizing the core problem, followed by selecting a strategy, executing it, and
finally evaluating the outcome.

question: Which of the following statements  are correct regarding the Incidence Rate?  1. It is useful for taking action to control a disease.  2. A rising incidence rate may indicate the ineffectiveness of current control  programs.  3. Fluctuation in the incidence rate may indicate a change in the etiology of  the disease. Select the correct answer:

options.d: Explanation & Tip: Incidence  measures new cases. If new cases are rising, it directly implies the  current prevention strategy is failing.  [Subject: PSM | Topic: Epidemiology / Disease Endemicity]

explanation:
Incidence
measures new cases. If new cases are rising, it directly implies the
current prevention strategy is failing.

question: A high level  of infection beginning early in life and affecting most of the child population,  leading to a state of equilibrium such that the adult population shows  evidence of the disease much less commonly than children. This describes  which type of endemicity?

options.b: Explanation & Tip: Internet Mnemonic Tip: HOLO =  Whole child population affected early on. Malaria in certain African regions is a  classic example of holoendemicity.  [Subject: PSM | Topic: Communicable Diseases]

explanation:
Internet Mnemonic Tip: HOLO =
Whole child population affected early on. Malaria in certain African regions is a
classic example of holoendemicity.

question: Consider the following  statements regarding the Dengue Virus:  1. It has four distinct serotypes (DEN-1, 2, 3, and 4).  2. Infection with any one serotype confers life-long immunity for that  specific serotype.  3. Secondary infection with Dengue serotype 2 leads to a more severe form  of Dengue. Which of the statements given above are correct?
explanation:
All
statements are correct. Secondary infection with a different serotype
(especially DEN-2) is highly dangerous due to Antibody-Dependent

28
For More High-Yield Medical
Notes & Updates
Follow & DM: @itsgauravmalikk
Enhancement (ADE), leading to Dengue Hemorrhagic Fever or Dengue
Shock Syndrome.

question: Consider the following  statements regarding the transmission of Dengue by the Aedes mosquito:  1. The mosquito becomes infective after an extrinsic incubation period of 8  to 10 days.  2. Once it becomes infective, the mosquito remains infective for life.  3. The genital tract of the mosquito does not get infected. Select the  correct answer:
explanation:
Statement 3 is incorrect. The
Dengue virus does infect the mosquito's genital tract, allowing for
Transovarial Transmission (the virus is passed directly to the mosquito's
eggs and the next generation).

question: A researcher has obtained country- level data on the Average Body Mass Index (BMI) and Average Sugar Intake  for 100 countries. Which among the following is the best way to represent  the relationship between BMI and Sugar Intake?
explanation:
Scatter
plots (dot diagrams) are the gold standard for showing the correlation between
two separate quantitative variables.

question: Front-line health  workers are employed to record births and deaths in a country that has made  slow progress in the development of a comprehensive vital registration  system. This approach is known as:

options.c: Explanation &  Tip: Lay reporting involves non-professional or front-line workers collecting  vital health data in areas where official medical/legal registration systems are  incomplete or inadequate.  [Subject: PSM | Topic: Demography]

explanation:
Lay reporting involves non-professional or front-line workers collecting
vital health data in areas where official medical/legal registration systems are
incomplete or inadequate.

question: While calculating the Total  Dependency Ratio of a population, which of the following is used in the  denominator?
explanation:
The denominator represents the economically

29
For More High-Yield Medical
Notes & Updates
Follow & DM: @itsgauravmalikk
active/productive population (15-64 years). The numerator consists of the
dependents (0-14 years AND >65 years).

question: As per the United  Nations definition of a Vital Event Registration System, which of the following  are included as vital events?  1. Fetal death  2. Legal separation  3. School admission  4. College graduation Select the correct answer:

options.a: Explanation & Tip: UN  vital events strictly include live birth, fetal death, marriage, divorce,  adoption, and legal separation. Educational milestones are excluded.  [Subject: PSM | Topic: Communicable Diseases]

explanation:
UN
vital events strictly include live birth, fetal death, marriage, divorce,
adoption, and legal separation. Educational milestones are excluded.

question: Consider the following  statements about Kyasanur Forest Disease (KFD):  1. It is transmitted by a tick.  2. It is originally called "Pig disease" because of its association with dead  pigs.  3. It was first recognized in Karnataka state. Which of the statements given  above are correct?

options.b: Explanation & Tip: Statement 2 is entirely incorrect.  It is known as Monkey Fever/Disease, not pig disease. Tip: KFD =  Karnataka Forest Disease.  [Subject: PSM | Topic: Immunization]

explanation:
Statement 2 is entirely incorrect.
It is known as Monkey Fever/Disease, not pig disease. Tip: KFD =
Karnataka Forest Disease.

question: In a country with a low level of  Measles transmission, what should be the appropriate age for starting  immunization with a measles-containing vaccine?

options.c: Explanation & Tip: In high-risk  countries like India, it is given at 9 months. However, in developed countries  with low transmission, it is deliberately delayed to 12 months to ensure  circulating maternal antibodies do not neutralize the live vaccine virus.  [Subject: PSM | Topic: Communicable Diseases]

explanation:
In high-risk
countries like India, it is given at 9 months. However, in developed countries
with low transmission, it is deliberately delayed to 12 months to ensure
circulating maternal antibodies do not neutralize the live vaccine virus.

question: Monkeypox, an  emerging viral zoonotic disease, is caused by which type of virus?
explanation:
30
For More High-Yield Medical
Notes & Updates
Follow & DM: @itsgauravmalikk
Monkeypox belongs to the Orthopoxvirus genus (like Smallpox), which are
large, double-stranded DNA (dsDNA) viruses.

question: Raju, a 6-year-old boy, was  returning home from school. At the gate, he was scratched by a stray dog and  there was some bleeding from the wound. Which post-exposure prophylaxis  protocol must be followed?

options.a: Wash the wound and observe the dog
options.b: Wash  the wound and administer the Rabies Vaccine only
options.d: Explanation & Tip: A scratch with bleeding  automatically classifies it as a Category 3 exposure. This absolute emergency  requires all three steps: 15-20 min soap/water wash, full vaccine course, and  RIG infiltration into the wound.  [Subject: PSM | Topic: Environmental Health]

explanation:
A scratch with bleeding
automatically classifies it as a Category 3 exposure. This absolute emergency
requires all three steps: 15-20 min soap/water wash, full vaccine course, and
RIG infiltration into the wound.

question: The method of combined  disposal of refuse and night soil by organic breakdown is known as:
explanation:
Composting is the decomposition of organic matter (refuse + night soil)
into a humus-like material, which is highly effective and agriculturally useful.

question: Consider the following  statements about the working of a Septic Tank:  1. They are recommended for large communities.  2. There should be no air space between the level of liquid in the tank and  the undersurface of the cover.  3. The liquid effluent from the outlet pipe is free from all pathogens. Which  of the statements given above are correct?
explanation:
Septic
tanks are designed for small populations/individual households. There
must be an air space (minimum 30 cm) between the liquid and the cover.
The effluent liquid is not free of pathogens; it still contains cysts and
bacteria requiring further soil absorption.

question: Which of the following amino acids  are categorized as Non-Essential Amino Acids?
explanation:
Internet Mnemonic
Tip: Essential Amino Acids = TV TILL PM (Tryptophan, Valine, Threonine,

31
For More High-Yield Medical
Notes & Updates
Follow & DM: @itsgauravmalikk
Isoleucine, Leucine, Lysine, Phenylalanine, Methionine). Amino acids like
Alanine and Glutamic acid are synthesized by the body (Non-Essential).

question: What is the correct descending order  (highest to lowest) regarding the Fat content per 100 grams of milk?
explanation:
Buffalo milk is the richest in fat (~7-8%), followed by
Goat/Sheep (~3-4%), Cow (~3-4%), and Human milk (~3-4% but generally
slightly lower fat density than bovine sources).

question: Which of the following are examples  of Unsaturated Fatty Acids?  1. Palmitic acid  2. Linoleic acid  3. Oleic acid  4. Stearic acid Select the correct answer:
explanation:
Palmitic and Stearic are
Saturated fats (single bonds). Oleic acid is Mono-unsaturated. Linoleic
acid is Poly-unsaturated.

question: In India, the most widely prevalent  stage of Iron Deficiency is:

options.a: Stage 1: Depletion of iron stores with normal  transferrin

explanation:
Latent Iron
Deficiency (Stage 2) where iron stores are exhausted and transferrin saturation
drops (but absolute anemia has not yet fully set in) is statistically the most
widely prevalent stage across the Indian population.

question: Consider the  following statements: Statement 1: Waist circumference and Waist-to-hip ratio  are more powerful predictors of the subsequent risk of Type 2 Diabetes than  Body Mass Index (BMI). Statement 2: Central obesity is a major determinant of  insulin resistance, the underlying abnormality in most cases of Type 2 Diabetes.  Select the correct answer:

options.a: Both Statement 1 and 2 are correct, and  Statement 2 is the correct explanation for Statement 1.
options.b: Both Statement 1  and 2 are correct, but Statement 2 is NOT the correct explanation for    32    For More High-Yield Medical  Notes & Updates  Follow & DM: @itsgauravmalikk  Statement 1.
options.c: Statement 1 is correct, but Statement 2 is incorrect.
options.d: Statement 1 is incorrect, but Statement 2 is correct.

explanation:
BMI does not distinguish between muscle and fat distribution. Waist
circumference specifically measures Central (Visceral) Obesity, which is directly
linked to severe insulin resistance.

question: Educating people to  preserve their traditional eating patterns and healthy lifestyles associated  with a low level of Coronary Heart Disease falls under which level of  prevention?
explanation:
• Primordial: Preventing the risk factor from even emerging (e.g.,
maintaining traditional healthy diets).
• Primary: The risk factor is present (e.g., obesity), but we prevent the
disease.

question: Raju is 30 years of age. He  has been working in the ceramic and pottery industry for the last 10 years.  Which of the following conditions should he be routinely screened for?

options.c: Explanation &  Tip: Researcher Tip:  • Pottery/Glass/Stone = Silicosis  • Cotton textiles = Byssinosis  • Coal mining = Anthracosis  • Sugarcane = Bagassosis  [Subject: PSM | Topic: Occupational Health]

explanation:
Researcher Tip:
• Pottery/Glass/Stone = Silicosis
• Cotton textiles = Byssinosis
• Coal mining = Anthracosis
• Sugarcane = Bagassosis

question: An industrial worker was  brought to a medical facility. Laboratory test findings showed the "Basophilic  Stippling" of red blood cells. What is the most likely diagnosis?
explanation:
Internet Mnemonic Tip: ABCDE of Lead
Poisoning: Anemia, Basophilic stippling/Burton's line, Colic/Constipation, Drop
(wrist/foot), Encephalopathy. Lead interferes with heme synthesis, leading to
ribosomal RNA aggregation (stippling).

question: Consider the following pairs  of Genetic Diseases and their Type of Inheritance:    33    For More High-Yield Medical  Notes & Updates  Follow & DM: @itsgauravmalikk  1. Cystic Fibrosis - Autosomal Recessive  2. Huntington's Chorea - Autosomal Dominant  3. Marfan Syndrome - X-linked Recessive How many of the above pairs are  correctly matched?
explanation:
Marfan
Syndrome is strictly an Autosomal Dominant connective tissue disorder
(fibrillin-1 defect), not X-linked. Cystic Fibrosis and Huntington's are
correctly matched.

question: Consider the  following statements: Statement 1: Signs of Thalassemia Major usually develop  after 6 months of age. Statement 2: This is the time when hemoglobin  synthesis switches from Fetal Hemoglobin (HbF) to Adult Hemoglobin (HbA).  Select the correct answer:

options.a: Both Statement 1 and 2 are correct, and  Statement 2 is the correct explanation for Statement 1.
options.b: Both Statement 1  and 2 are correct, but Statement 2 is NOT the correct explanation for  Statement 1.
options.c: Statement 1 is correct, but Statement 2 is incorrect.
options.d: Statement 1 is incorrect, but Statement 2 is correct.

explanation:
Thalassemia is a defect in adult hemoglobin (HbA). Because newborns
primarily rely on Fetal Hemoglobin (HbF) for the first 6 months, the disease
symptoms remain masked until the physiological switch to HbA occurs.

question: The "Weekly  Epidemiological Record" is an essential publication released by which  organization?
explanation:
The WHO publishes the Weekly Epidemiological Record (WER) globally.
The Morbidity and Mortality Weekly Report (MMWR) is published by the CDC.

question: As per the basic model of the nuclear  family life cycle, what is the correct chronological sequence of phases?

Extension: Birth of children.  3. Contraction: Children grow up and leave home.  4. Dissolution: Death of a spouse.  [Subject: PSM | Topic: Health Communication]

explanation:
Mnemonic Tip: Families Expect Continuous Devotion.
1. Formation: Marriage.

34
For More High-Yield Medical
Notes & Updates
Follow & DM: @itsgauravmalikk
2. Extension: Birth of children.
3. Contraction: Children grow up and leave home.
4. Dissolution: Death of a spouse.

question: Which among the  following is the primary characteristic of "Health Education" as opposed to  "Propaganda"?

options.a: It forces a specific behavioral change through emotional  manipulation
options.b: It aims to empower and educate individuals to make their  own informed choices
options.c: It appeals directly to primitive desires
options.d: It is heavily  biased and misleading

explanation:
Education builds
knowledge and empowers voluntary, rational decision-making (Knowledge-
centered). Propaganda dictates behavior using biased, misleading, or
emotional messaging.

question: Consider the following  statements regarding the LNG-20 (Mirena) Intrauterine Device:  1. It is a T-shaped IUD filled with a natural hormone (Progesterone).  2. It is associated with a very low pregnancy rate.  3. It is associated with a large number of ectopic pregnancies.  4. It is associated with lower menstrual blood loss. Select the correct  answer:
explanation:
It contains synthetic Levonorgestrel (LNG), not natural
progesterone. Because it provides excellent overall contraception, it
actively decreases the overall absolute risk of an ectopic pregnancy. The
progestin thins the endometrium, effectively treating heavy bleeding
(menorrhagia).

question: What is the  most common cause of Maternal Mortality in India?
explanation:
Obstetric hemorrhage (specifically Postpartum
Hemorrhage - PPH) remains the leading cause of maternal mortality both in
India and globally.

question: In the National Malaria  Control Program, Indoor Residual Spraying (IRS) is the primary method of  vector control in rural settings. Which of the following is the insecticide of    35    For More High-Yield Medical  Notes & Updates  Follow & DM: @itsgauravmalikk  choice?
explanation:
While Malathion is also used, Dichloro-diphenyl-
trichloroethane (DDT) has historically been and remains the primary insecticide
of choice for IRS in rural malaria control programs in India due to its long
residual effect.

question: Which of the  following statements are correct with regard to the selection of an Accredited  Social Health Activist (ASHA)?  1. ASHA must be a woman preferably in the age group of 18 to 25 years.  2. ASHA must have had formal education strictly up to class 10.  3. ASHA must be a resident of the village.  4. ASHA may be married, widowed, or divorced. Select the correct answer:
explanation:
Age preference is 25 to 45 years (not 18-25). While Class 10 is
preferred, formal education up to Class 8 is also widely accepted,
meaning it is not strictly Class 10. She must be a resident, and
married/widow/divorced status is preferred to ensure stability in the
village.

question: Consider the  following statements regarding the Central Drugs Standard Control  Organization (CDSCO):  1. It is headed by the Drug Controller General of India (DCGI).  2. It is a part of the Directorate General of Health Services (DGHS).  3. It falls under the Ministry of Chemicals and Fertilizers.  4. It has a network of several port offices for monitoring the import and  export of drugs. Which of the statements given above are correct?
explanation:
Statement 3 is false. The CDSCO is the national
regulatory body for Indian pharmaceuticals and operates directly under
the Ministry of Health and Family Welfare, not Chemicals and Fertilizers.

question: The National  AIDS Control Organization (NACO) provides pre-packed color-coded STI/RTI  kits. Which of the following matches is CORRECT?    36    For More High-Yield Medical  Notes & Updates  Follow & DM: @itsgauravmalikk  1. Red Kit - Urethral Discharge  2. Green Kit - Vaginitis / Vaginal Discharge  3. White Kit - Genital Ulcer Select the correct answer:
explanation:
Mnemonic Tip:
• Grey: Urethral discharge.
• Green: Vaginitis / Vaginal Discharge.
• Red / Blue / White: Various types of Genital Ulcers (White is specifically
for non-herpetic, but as per the exam source context, only Green
perfectly matches).
• Yellow: Lower abdominal pain.
• Black: Inguinal Bubo.

question: In an ELISA test  screening for HIV, out of 5,000 truly infected individuals, the test showed  4,900 as positive. Out of 95,000 truly non-infected individuals, the test  correctly showed 94,050 as negative. What is the Sensitivity and Specificity of  this test?
explanation:
• Sensitivity = (True Positives / Total Truly Infected) * 100 = (4,900 / 5,000)
* 100 = 98%.
• Specificity = (True Negatives / Total Truly Non-infected) * 100 = (94,050 /
95,000) * 100 = 99%.

question: Which of the  following is CORRECT regarding the Arginine Vasopressin antagonist  Tolvaptan?

options.b: It is useful in hypovolemic  hyponatremia
options.c: It should be used as chronic therapy for at least one year
options.d: It is an oral drug that specifically antagonizes the V2 receptor

explanation:
Vaptans (like Tolvaptan) are oral drugs that selectively block V2 receptors in
the kidney to cause water diuresis. They are strictly contraindicated in
hypovolemic hyponatremia because they worsen volume depletion, leading to
shock. Chronic therapy beyond a month is contraindicated due to severe liver
toxicity.

question: Which condition is  caused by mutations in the gene encoding the Sodium-Potassium-2-Chloride  (NKCC2) cotransporter, leading to sodium wasting, hypokalemia,  hypomagnesemia, and hypercalciuria?
explanation:
The
NKCC2 channel is located in the thick ascending limb of the Loop of Henle,
exactly where Loop Diuretics act. Internet Mnemonic Tip: "Loop diuretics LOSE
calcium" -> Hypercalciuria -> Bartter Syndrome. (Thiazides retain calcium ->
Hypocalciuria -> Gitelman Syndrome).

question: In a 52-year-old  male presenting with uncontrolled diabetes, which test will optimally help in  the early detection of diabetic nephropathy?

options.c: |  Tip: Microalbuminuria is the absolute earliest sensitive marker of kidney  damage in a diabetic patient. Serum creatinine and shrunken kidneys on  ultrasound strictly appear much later in the disease progression.  [Subject: Medicine | Topic: Nephrology / Genetics]

explanation:
Microalbuminuria is the absolute earliest sensitive marker of kidney
damage in a diabetic patient. Serum creatinine and shrunken kidneys on
ultrasound strictly appear much later in the disease progression.

question: Autosomal dominant  mutations in which one of the following genes cause a syndrome featuring  Focal Segmental Glomerulosclerosis (FSGS), abnormal genitalia, Wilms tumor,  and mental retardation?
explanation:
This exactly describes WAGR Syndrome (Wilms tumor, Aniridia,

question: According to the KDIGO  classification, kidney damage with a Glomerular Filtration Rate (GFR) value  strictly between 15 to 29 mL/min/1.73m2 is classified as:
explanation:

(Kidney failure requiring dialysis/transplant). KDIGO also utilizes Albuminuria
staging (A1, A2, A3) for early detection.

question: Which one of the  following hereditary tubulointerstitial kidney diseases strictly possesses an  Autosomal Recessive mode of inheritance?

options.c: | Tip: Nephronophthisis  involves NPHP gene mutations, is inherited purely recessively, and is a leading  cause of pediatric end-stage renal disease. The other options are Autosomal  Dominant.  [Subject: Medicine | Topic: Nephrology]

explanation:

question: Which of the following findings  in a patient are highly suggestive of Acute Nephritis?  1. Reduced size of both kidneys. 2. Hematuria. 3. Decreased renal output.  4. Swelling of kidneys. Select the correct statements:
explanation:
Shrunken, bilateral small kidneys
are the absolute classic hallmark of Chronic Kidney Disease (CKD), not
acute nephritis. Acute inflammation causes the kidneys to swell, bleed
(hematuria), and drop output.

question: A 35-year-old  male presents with polyuria (urine output > 4 Liters/day) and noticeably LOW  urine osmolality (< 250 mOsm/L). Which of the following conditions could be  the cause?  1. question: Contralateral  homonymous UPPER quadrantanopia is a visual field defect localized to a  lesion in the:
explanation:
The temporal lobe is responsible for the upper visual
field. Internet Mnemonic Tip: "Pie in the SKY" = Temporal lobe lesion. ("Pie on

question: Which of the following  statements is CORRECT regarding Migraines?  1. Common migraine (without aura) accounts for 80% of cases. 2. Classical  migraine comes with an aura (mostly visual, e.g., fortification spectra). 3.  Vasoconstrictors (Triptans) are used in management. Select the correct  answer:
explanation:
Migraine is caused by the extreme dilation of extracranial blood
vessels. Triptans (like Sumatriptan) are powerful vasoconstrictors used to
instantly reverse this dilation. The visual aura is called "Fortification
Spectra" (shimmering zigzag lines).

question: A 50-year-old man presents with  brief, intense episodes of unilateral, lancinating (electric-shock like) facial  pain perfectly localized in the buccal distribution. What is the diagnosis?

explanation:
Lancinating, brief electric-
shock facial pain triggered by chewing, talking, or cold wind is the screaming
hallmark of Trigeminal Neuralgia.

question: Which of the following heart  sounds or murmurs are physically BEST heard using the BELL of a  stethoscope?  1. Third Heart Sound (S3). 2. Fourth Heart Sound (S4). 3. Opening Snap. 4.  Systolic Clicks. Select the correct statements:
explanation:
The BELL is strictly used for low-
pitched sounds (S3, S4, Mid-diastolic murmurs of MS, Carey Coombs,
Austin Flint). The DIAPHRAGM is used for loud/high-pitched sounds (S1,
S2, Opening Snaps, Systolic Clicks).

question: In a patient with a  recent Myocardial Infarction, which triad of findings strongly points toward  the early development of a Ventricular Aneurysm?

options.a: Tachycardia, pulsus  paradoxus, and narrow pulse pressure
options.c: Diffuse ST depression, bradycardia, and a normal X- Ray
options.d: Hypotension, muffled heart sounds, and raised JVP
options.b: | Tip: If  the ST segment fails to settle down after an MI and remains persistently  elevated alongside a bulging chest X-ray, the weakened dead muscle has  ballooned out into an aneurysm.  [Subject: Medicine | Topic: Rheumatology / Cardiology]

explanation:
If
the ST segment fails to settle down after an MI and remains persistently
elevated alongside a bulging chest X-ray, the weakened dead muscle has
ballooned out into an aneurysm.

question: Which of the  following statements is CORRECT regarding the Subcutaneous Nodules seen  in Acute Rheumatic Fever?

options.a: They are present over the flexor aspects of the  forearm.
options.c: Their usual size is large, between 3  to 5 cm.
options.d: They typically appear more than 3 weeks after the onset of other  clinical manifestations.

explanation:
Rheumatic nodules are strictly small
(0.5 - 2 cm), completely PAINLESS, and found firmly attached to the extensor
surfaces of bones/tendons, appearing late in the disease.

question: The "Carey Coombs Murmur" is  clinically characterized by:

options.c: An early diastolic murmur of aortic  regurgitation

explanation:
Acute rheumatic fever
inflames the mitral valve leaflets (valvulitis), causing turbulent blood flow that
creates this highly specific low-pitched rumble.

question: Regarding the "Opening Snap"  seen in Mitral Stenosis, which statement is CORRECT?

options.a: It is best heard with  the bell of the stethoscope.
options.b: It is best heard at the second left intercostal  space.
options.c: Its intensity becomes far louder when the valve gets heavily calcified.

explanation:
A very tight, severe stenosis causes the left
atrial pressure to skyrocket. This massive pressure forces the stiff valve to snap
open much earlier in diastole, right after S2. If the valve becomes completely
calcified (stone-like), it stops moving entirely and the snap becomes entirely
inaudible.

question: What is the  expected physiological response when an IV bolus of Adenosine is  administered for a Paroxysmal Supraventricular Tachycardia (PSVT)?
explanation:
Adenosine blocks the AV node. In PSVT (like AVNRT), the AV node is the actual
engine of the circuit. Blocking it instantly terminates the attack. (In Atrial
Fibrillation/Flutter, it only causes a transient AV block because the engine is in
the atria).

question: What is the CORRECT  sequential order of steps for appropriately using a Meter Dose Inhaler (MDI)?  1. Simultaneously begin a slow deep inspiration, depress the canister, and  continue to inhale. 2. Remove the cap and shake the inhaler. 3. Incline  the head slightly backward. 4. Breathe out gently and place the  mouthpiece into the mouth. 5. Hold the breath for 10 seconds. Select  the correct sequence:
explanation:
The head MUST be tilted backward just
before firing to ensure the medicine blasts straight down the trachea
instead of smashing into the back of the throat (minimizing
oropharyngeal deposition).

question: A 62-year-old chronic smoker  is diagnosed with COPD. Spirometry testing reveals an FEV1/FVC ratio of 0.60,  and an FEV1 that is exactly 70% of the predicted value. Under the GOLD  criteria, what is the severity of his airflow obstruction?
explanation:
FEV1/FVC < 0.70 proves obstruction. If FEV1 is >= 80% (Mild); 50-79%
(Moderate); 30-49% (Severe); <30% (Very Severe). 70% falls squarely in the
Moderate category.

question: What is the exact Mendelian mode  of inheritance shared by both Cystic Fibrosis and Wilson's Disease?
explanation:
These are classic Autosomal Recessive
conditions. Internet Mnemonic Tip: "PASTA CHAWAL" (Phenylketonuria,
Albinism, Sickle cell, Thalassemia, Cystic fibrosis, Hemochromatosis, Wilson's,
Lysosomal storage).

question: Non-Invasive  Positive Pressure Ventilation (NIPPV/BiPAP) is absolutely CONTRAINDICATED  in patients exhibiting:  1. Cardiovascular instability (shock). 2. Impaired mental status /  Uncooperativeness. 3. Copious un-clearable respiratory secretions. 4.  Extreme craniofacial abnormalities or burns. Select the correct answer:

explanation:

of protecting their own airway. If they have massive secretions or a
burned face, you must forcefully intubate them instead.

question: Which one of the  following childhood epileptic disorders characteristically carries a massive  risk of relapse and heavily requires long-term anti-epileptic treatment?

question: Which of the following is an  expected, classical clinical feature of Parkinson's Disease?
explanation:
The
patient's handwriting physically shrinks and becomes cramped (Micrographia)
as they write across the page due to severe basal ganglia bradykinesia.

question: A 35-year-old female presents  with severe easy fatigability. Investigations reveal a profoundly low  Hemoglobin level, normocytic-normochromic red cell morphology, and a  Reticulocyte Production Index (RPI) of 5.5. What is the diagnosis?
explanation:
An
RPI > 2.0 proves the bone marrow is hyperactive and desperately pumping out
baby RBCs (reticulocytes) to replace blood that is either bleeding out or actively
being destroyed (Hemolysis). RPI < 2.0 signifies marrow failure
(deficiencies/aplastic).

question: A 46-year-old lady  adhering to a strict, long-term vegan diet presents with tingling, numbness in  her lower limbs, and marked swaying while walking (ataxia). What is the  primary cause?
explanation:
Plants contain absolute
ZERO Vitamin B12. Vegans inevitably destroy their posterior spinal cord
columns (causing ataxia and numbness) if they do not artificially supplement
B12.

question: A healthy patient presents with  "Relative Erythrocytosis" (a spuriously high RBC count despite absolutely  normal red cell mass) strictly due to extreme plasma volume depletion driven  by smoking, obesity, or diuretic use. This condition is historically termed:
explanation:

question: Regarding the step-wise  management of Idiopathic Thrombocytopenic Purpura (ITP), which  statements are CORRECT?  1. explanation:
ITP is an
autoimmune platelet massacre. Steroids suppress the immune system. If
bleeding is catastrophic, slam IVIG to distract the macrophages and

question: Which of the  following drug classes notoriously cause pathological "Secondary Weight  Gain" as a major side effect?  1. Insulin and Sulfonylureas. 2. Glucocorticoids. 3. Beta-blockers. 4. Atypical  Antipsychotics and Amitriptyline. Select the correct answer:
explanation:
Insulin is massively
lipogenic (drives fat storage). Glucocorticoids cause Cushingoid central
obesity. Psychiatric drugs alter metabolism. (Metformin and Thyroxine
cause weight LOSS).

question: Hypothalamic-Pituitary- Adrenal (HPA) axis suppression resulting in a catastrophic Adrenal Crisis is  highly likely if Glucocorticoids are suddenly withdrawn after:

options.a: Being given  IV for only 5 days
options.b: Being administered orally for only one week
options.c: | Tip: Chronic steroids  literally put the patient's natural adrenal glands to sleep. Sudden withdrawal  leaves the body with ZERO cortisol, causing a fatal shock crisis. The dose must  be tapered over weeks.  [Subject: Medicine | Topic: Critical Care / Nephrology]

explanation:
Chronic steroids
literally put the patient's natural adrenal glands to sleep. Sudden withdrawal
leaves the body with ZERO cortisol, causing a fatal shock crisis. The dose must
be tapered over weeks.

question: Standard  emergency protocol for managing severe, symptomatic Hyperkalemia  includes the administration of:  1. IV 10% Calcium Gluconate. 2. IV Sodium Bicarbonate. 3. Oral/Rectal  Sodium Polystyrene Sulfonate. 4. IV Calcitonin (Wrong). Select the  correct answer:
explanation:
Calcium Gluconate physically stabilizes the cardiac cell membrane to
stop fatal arrhythmias. Bicarbonate (alongside Insulin/Beta-agonists)
shoves potassium deep into the cells. Polystyrene Sulfonate permanently
binds it in the gut and excretes it in stool.

question: Euvolemic  Hyponatremia (low sodium with perfectly normal body fluid volume) is  clinically caused by:
explanation:
Hypothyroidism and SIADH do
not physically swell the body with edema (hypervolemic) nor dehydrate it
(hypovolemic); they simply dilute the sodium.

question: A Pleural Fluid Analysis  reveals a Fluid Protein to Serum Protein ratio of 0.08 (Less than 0.5) and a  Fluid LDH to Serum LDH ratio of 0.33 (Less than 0.6). What is the most likely  underlying diagnosis causing this effusion?
explanation:
Because it violently fails Light's Criteria (<0.5 and <0.6), the fluid is perfectly
clean filtered water (a Transudate). Transudates are strictly caused by heart,
kidney, or liver failure, plus Myxedema. (The others are inflammatory
Exudates).

question: A lung biopsy  from a patient with a restrictive lung defect reveals distinctive polypoid plugs  of loosely arranged organizing connective tissue inside the alveolar ducts.  These are pathologically known as "Masson Bodies". Diagnosis?

question: Regarding  expected respiratory physiological changes in healthy older adults, which  statements are TRUE?  1. There is an increased Ventilation-Perfusion (V/Q) mismatch. 2. There is a  marked decline in FEV1 and FVC. 3. There is a decline in maximum  oxygen capacity. 4. There is an increased ventilatory response to hypoxia  (Wrong). Select the correct answer:
explanation:
Aging lungs lose their elastic recoil. Because the
brain's sensors age as well, the elderly have a drastically REDUCED
physiological panic response to dropping oxygen levels (hypoxia).

question: Regarding the  severe Pulmonary involvement commonly seen in Rheumatoid Arthritis (RA),  which statements are CORRECT?  1. It is more common in men. 2. Pleural effusions are typically small,  unilateral Exudates containing markedly low glucose and high LDH. 3.  The combination of RA nodules and pneumoconiosis is termed Caplan  Syndrome. Select the correct answer:
explanation:
The most common lung complication of RA is
interstitial pulmonary fibrosis (specifically NSIP pattern). If a miner with
RA inhales coal dust, the lungs erupt into massive destructive nodules
(Caplan Syndrome).

DPT/HepB vaccines directly on the floor of the fridge, they will instantly
freeze, shatter the proteins, and become totally useless. Baskets
physically keep them elevated in the safe zone.

question: Keshan disease is due to deficiency of
explanation:
Deficiency of selenium has resulted in Keshan
disease (a form of cardiomyopathy endemic in China) and Kashin-Beck
disease (an endemic osteoarthritis of preadolescents in China). Other dietary
factors such as low intake of vitamin E, protein, and methionine are also
considered relevant to causation of Keshan disease.

A four-fold increase in the titer obtained in Weil-Felix reaction is  diagnostic of
explanation:
• Rickettsial bacteria are obligate intracellular parasites, Gram-negative
coccobacilli, and short bacilli that grow strictly in eukaryotic cells
(unable to grow in cell media). Tissue cultures are not satisfactory for
primary isolation.
• Rickettsial zoonoses are a group of specific communicable diseases
caused by rickettsial organisms and transmitted to man by arthropod
vectors (except Q fever).
• In Rocky Mountain spotted fever—red macular rash appears between
the second and sixth days of fever, first on the wrists and ankles and
then spreading centrally, it may become petechial, involvement of
palms and soles is characteristic.
• Scrub typhus—one typical feature is punched out ulcer covered with a
blackened scab (eschar) which indicates the location of the mite bite.
• Weil-Felix reaction—for serodiagnosis of typhus fever is a heterophile
agglutination test and is based on the sharing of a common antigen
between typhus rickettsiae and some strains of proteus bacilli. The
Weil-Felix reaction is a simple and useful test for the diagnosis of some
rickettsial diseases. The Ab appears rapidly during the course of
disease, reaches peak titers of up to 1:1,000 or 1:5,000 by 2nd week,
and declines rapidly during convalescence. It does not differentiate
between epidemic and endemic typhus and is of no value in rickettsial
pox, trench fever, and Q fever. [Mnemonic—QRST. Q fever, rickettsial
pox, scrub typhus, trench fever—all, negative with OX19, OX2, OXK
except in scrub fever positive with OXK].

• Doxycycline is the drug of choice for specific treatment of all rickettsial
diseases. In pregnant female chloramphenicol may be given. Other
alternative for scrub typhus is azithromycin, endemic typhus is
ciprofloxacin, Josamycin for tick born spotted fever.

question: Most common strain of E. coli giving rise to traveler's diarrhea is

options.c: Explanation: Diarrhea caused by E. coli—some strains of E. coli  which produce diarrhea from enterotoxins. Two types of E. coli enterotoxins  have been identified.

explanation:
Diarrhea caused by E. coli—some strains of E. coli
which produce diarrhea from enterotoxins. Two types of E. coli enterotoxins
have been identified. (a) Heat labile—large molecular weight resembles the
cholera enterotoxin antigenically, acts by stimulating the adenyl cyclase
cyclic-adenosine monophosphate (cAMP) system. (b) Heat stable—low
molecular weight, nonantigenic, acts through cGMP.
• Enteropathogenic E. coli—infantile diarrhea (pediatric)
• Enterotoxicogenic E. coli—traveler's diarrhea (most common)
• Enteroinvasive E. coli—Invades the intestinal epithelium as do
dysentery bacilli, mistaken for shigellosis. Sereny test—positive
• Enterohemorrhagic E. coli—Hemorrhagic colitis and HUS. E. coli
0157:H7. Cytotoxin appears identical to shigella shiga toxin. Called
"verotoxin". It differs from the disease caused EIEC in that fever is not
always present, but hemorrhage is marked.

Consider the following statements with reference to HIV:  1. HIV belongs to lentivirus group of the retrovirus family.  2. HIV-associated lymphomas are very slowly progressive.  3. HIV infection is diagnosed by using Western blot method.  4. Following an HIV infection, the person immediately shows the  symptoms of the disease.  5. The interaction between CD4 and HIV surface glycoprotein together  with chemokine coreceptors like CCR5 is responsible for HIV entry into  the cells. Which of the above statements are correct?
explanation:
• Family Retroviridae has 3 subfamilies—
1. Oncoviridae—HTLV-1
2. Lentiviridae—HIV
3. Spumaviridae—foamy virus CMDT:
• The virus can infect all cells expressing the T4 (CD4) antigen, which HIV
uses to attach to the cells. Chemokine receptors (CCR5 and CXCR4) are
important for virus import and individuals with CCR5 deletion are less
likely to become infected.
• The mean time of approximately 10 years between exposure and
development of AIDS.
• Non-Hodgkin's lymphoma in HIV-infected person tends to be very
aggressive, usually B-cell origin and characterized by diffuse large-cell
tumor and extranodal (>70%).
• ELISA—screening test, sensitivity >99.9%, 50% positive within 22 days
and 95% are positive within 6 weeks after transmission. Western blot—
confirmatory test specificity when combined with ELISA—>99.99%.

options.b: Explanation: Treatment of acute salicylate poisoning— symptomatic and supportive. Most important is external cooling and IV fluid  with Na+, K+, HCO3-, and glucose according to need determined by repeated  monitoring. Gastric lavage to remove unabsorbed drug, forced alkaline  diuresis, or hemodialysis to remove absorbed drug is indicated in severe  cases. Blood transfusion and vitamin K should be given if bleeding occurs.

explanation:
Treatment of acute salicylate poisoning—
symptomatic and supportive. Most important is external cooling and IV fluid
with Na+, K+, HCO3-, and glucose according to need determined by repeated
monitoring. Gastric lavage to remove unabsorbed drug, forced alkaline
diuresis, or hemodialysis to remove absorbed drug is indicated in severe
cases. Blood transfusion and vitamin K should be given if bleeding occurs.

question: The chances of acquiring HIV infection following needle prick is
explanation:
HIV risk for health-care professionals:
• The risk of HIV transmission from a needle stick with blood from an
HIV-infected patient is about 0.3% and 0.09% after a mucous
membrane exposure. The risk is high with deep puncture, large inocula
with high viral load source.
• The risk of hepatitis B infection following similar type of exposure—
20% to 30%.
• Risk of acquiring HIV infection from illicit drug use with sharing of
needle from HIV-infected source is estimated 1:150.
• When blood transfusion from an HIV-infected donor occurs, the risk of
transmission is 95%.
• Heterosexual transmission is the most common mode of infection.
• Risk of HIV transmission with (i) receptive anal intercourse is between
1:100 and 1:30; (ii) with insertive anal intercourse 1:1,000; (iii) with
insertive vaginal intercourse 1:10,000; and (iv) with receptive fellatio
with ejaculation—1:1,000.
• With few exceptions, the level of CD4+ T cell in blood decreases
gradually and progressively in HIV-infected individuals. The level of
viremia generally increases as disease progresses.
• Postexposure prophylaxis for HIV refers to antiretroviral drug
treatment started within hours following accidental exposure.

question: Consider the following statements with reference to poliomyelitis:  1. The incubation period of poliomyelitis is 4 to 35 days, but usually it lies  between 7 and 14 days.  2. Tendon reflexes are normal but sensations are not intact.  3. Paralysis due to heavy metal poisoning can be easily distinguished  clinically from poliomyelitis.  4. The histopathologic findings in poliomyelitis include necrosis of  neuronal cells and perivascular cuffing.  5. The major advantages of oral polio vaccine include ease of  administration and secondary immunization of nonimmune contacts in  the population. Which of the above statements are correct?
explanation:
• Incubation period of poliomyelitis—usually 7–14 days (range, 3–35
days).
• Paralytic polio occurs in less than 1% of infections, asymmetrical flaccid
paralysis, characterized as descending, proximal muscle group are more
involved. Deep tendon reflexes are diminished before the onset of
paralysis. There is no sensory loss.
• Robbins pathology—acute cases show mononuclear cell, perivascular
cuffs, and neuronophagia of the anterior horn motor neurons of the
spinal cord.
• Advantages of OPV—(i) since given orally, it is easy to administer, (ii)
induces both humoral and intestinal immunity, (iii) antibody is quickly
produced in large proportion of vaccines, even in single dose elicits
(except in tropical countries) substantial immunity, (iv) vaccine excretes
the virus and so infects others who are also immunized thereby, (v)
useful in controlling epidemics, (vi) relatively inexpensive.

explanation:
The rationale for laparoscopic cholecystectomy—(a)
good cosmetic result, (b) rapid healing of operative wound, (c) reduction of
hospital stay, (d) ability to return to work soon after surgery. Laparoscopic
cholecystectomy is the treatment of choice for gallstone disease. Main
drawback of the technique is the increased incidence of bile duct injury
compared with open cholecystectomy.

question: Assertion (A): Angiotensin-converting enzyme (ACE) inhibitors are  indicated for patients of acute myocardial infarction with <40% left  ventricular ejection fraction. 2.5–5 mg twice daily) can counteract ventricular remodeling and prevent the
onset of heart failure and indicate that this form of therapy should be
considered in any patient who has sustained a myocardial infarction

explanation:
• Anterior fossa fracture—subconjunctival hematoma, anosmia,
epistaxis, CSF rhinorrhea. Subgaleal hemorrhage—periorbital
ecchymoses (raccoon eye).
• Middle fossa fracture—CSF otorrhea or rhinorrhea via eustachian tube,

with petrous temporal base of skull fracture or VII and VIII cranial
nerve palsies.

question: Arterial blood supply to Little's area is from the following except

options.d: superior labial artery—septal branch. Greatest contribution from  sphenopalatine artery, which is often, termed "artery of epistaxis" and next  common—anterior ethmoid artery.

explanation:
Little's area—situated in anteroinferior part of
septum. This vascular area is a common site for epistaxis due to trauma or
nose-picking. This vascular plexus is called Kiesselbach's plexus. The vessels
which anastomose here—(a) anterior ethmoidal artery—septal branch, (b)
greater palatine artery—terminal branch, (c) sphenopalatine artery—septal
branch, (d) superior labial artery—septal branch. Greatest contribution from
sphenopalatine artery, which is often, termed "artery of epistaxis" and next
common—anterior ethmoid artery.

question: Which one of the following drugs does not affect accommodation?
explanation:
Action of cholinergic agonist on eye
1. Choline esters (acetylcholine, methacholine, carbachol, bethanechol)
and alkaloids (muscarine, pilocarpine, arecoline). Contraction of
circular muscle of iris—miosis. Contraction of ciliary muscle—spasm of
accommodation, outflow, ↓IOT.
2. Action of anticholinergic drugs (muscarinic receptor antagonists,
atropine, parasympatholytic) on eye—topical instillation of atropine
causes mydriasis, abolition of light reflex and cycloplegia lasting 7–10
days. This results in photophobia, blurring of near vision. The ciliary
muscles recover earlier than sphincter pupillae. IOT rises in narrow
angle glaucoma.
3. Anticholinesterases (anti-ChEs) are agents that inhibit ChE, protect ACh
from hydrolysis—produce cholinergic effect in vivo and potentiate ACh
both in vivo and in vitro.

A 17-year-old boy is admitted to hospital after road traffic accident. Per  abdomen examination is normal. explanation:
• Blunt hepatic injuries in hemodynamic stable patients without other
indications for explorations best served by conservative approach.

hemodynamic stability, normal mental status, absence of a clear
indication for laparotomy such as peritoneal signs, low-grade liver
injuries (grades 1 and 3), and transfusion required for less than 2 units
of blood.

Capsular tear, 1–3 cm parenchymal depth <10 cm in length | | III | Hematoma
| Subcapsular, >50% surface area of ruptured subcapsular or parenchymal

question: A 1 cm x 1 cm squamous cell carcinoma of anal canal is best treated  initially by

options.c: Proximal colostomy followed by interstitial irradiation

explanation:
• Treatment of tumors of anal verge—for squamous cell lesion—wide
local excision leaving a margin of at least 2.5 cm of tissue + if inguinal
nodes involved, block dissection removal.
• Treatment of tumors of anal canal—a combination of chemotherapy
and radiotherapy, so-called "chemoradiation"—preferred initial
therapy. Chemotherapy for 1 week (5 FU + mitomycin or bleomycin +
cisplantinum + adriamycin) followed by radiotherapy given over 3–7
weeks, if 4–6 weeks after cessation of treatment, obvious tumor
remaining → abdominoperineal excision.

question: The most useful investigation for profuse lower gastrointestinal bleeding  is
explanation:
Acute lower GI bleeding flow: If No hemodynamic
instability: -> Age <40 years: Flexible Sigmoidoscopy (Colonoscopy if familial
colon cancer, iron deficiency anemia, copious bleeding) -> Age >40 years:
Colonoscopy. If bleeding stops, consider enteroscopy, video capsule,
enteroclysis, angiography if major blood loss. If Hemodynamic instability: ->
Upper GI endoscopy to rule out an upper GI source -> Colonoscopy. -> If Mild
to moderate bleeding persists: Enteroscopy -> Video capsule -> Consider
intraoperative endoscopy. -> If Severe bleeding persists: Angiography (Tc-RBC
scan may be done first) -> Enteroscopy -> Intraoperative endoscopy.

Respiratory alkalosis is commonly seen in
explanation:
Acid-base and electrolyte balance of aspirin—
salicylates stimulate respiratory center → hyperventilation and hyperpnea, →
respiratory alkalosis + compensated alkaluria. Both potassium and sodium
bicarbonates are excreted in urine. On still higher doses → respiratory
depression → CO2 retention → respiratory acidosis. To this is added
dissociated salicylic acid as well as metabolic acids (lactic, pyruvic,
acetoacetic) which are produced in excess + metabolically derived sulfuric
and phosphoric acid which retained due to depression of renal function →
uncompensated metabolic acidosis (since plasma HCO3 already low). Most
children manifest this phase during salicylate poisoning while in adults it is
seen in late stages of poisoning only.

question: Which one of the following investigations is most sensitive for early  diagnosis of diabetic nephropathy?
explanation:
First stage of diabetic nephropathy is hyperfiltration
with an increase in GFR followed by development of microalbuminuria (30–
150 μg/min or 30–300 mg/day).

question: Mechanism of action of sildenafil is

options.d: Direct action on vascular smooth muscles

explanation:
Sildenafil—orally active drug for treatment of
erectile dysfunction.
1. Mode of action—selectively inhibiting phosphodiesterase-5 (PDE-5).
Since NO causes smooth muscle relaxation through cGMP, inhibition of
PDE-5 (the cGMP degrading isoenzyme in cavernosal and vascular
smooth muscle) by sildenafil enhances NO action, especially in corpus
cavernosum and increases penile tumescence during sexual arousal.
2. Pharmacokinetics—oral absorption 40%, peak blood level attained in 1
hour.
3. Adverse effect—headache, nasal congestion, dizziness, flushing, fall in
BP, loose motion, impairment of color discrimination.
4. Contraindications—coronary heart disease and those taking nitrates.
5. Cautions—liver disease, kidney disease, peptic ulcer, bleeding disorder,
patient of leukemia, sickle-cell anemia, myeloma.
6. Drug interaction—because it is metabolized by CYP3A4, inhibitors like
erythromycin, ketoconazole, verapamil, and cimetidine potentiate its
action.

question: Megaloblastic anemia may be caused by the following except
explanation:
Drugs causing megaloblastic anemia:
1. Direct inhibitors of DNA synthesis—purine analogs (6-thioguanine,
azathioprine, 6-mercaptopurine), pyrimidine analogs (5-fluorouracil,
cytosine arabinoside), hydroxyurea, procarbazine, zidovudine.
2. Folate antagonists—methotrexate, pentamidine, trimethoprim,
triamterene, pyrimethamine.
3. Others—nitrous oxide, phenytoin, primidone, phenobarbitone.

question: Massive splenomegaly is a feature of the following conditions, except
explanation:
Causes of massive splenomegaly—chronic
myelogenous leukemia (CML), lymphoma, hairy cell leukemia, myelofibrosis

Which one of the following is not a feature of Sturge-Weber syndrome  (encephalotrigeminal syndrome)?
explanation:
Sturge-Weber syndrome—when a port wine stain is
localized to trigeminal area of face, especially around the eyelids, the
diagnosis of Sturge-Weber syndrome (ipsilateral glaucoma, leptomeningeal
venous angioma, mental retardation, seizures, hemiparesis contralateral to
facial lesion, intracranial calcification) must be considered.
• Facial nevus—present at birth, unilateral and always involves upper
face and eyelids.
• Seizure—focal tonic-clonic, contralateral to facial nevus, mostly during
1st year of life (Jacksonian epilepsy).
• Skull radiograph—intracranial calcification (serpentine or railroad track
appearance) in occipitoparietal region.
• MRI—most sensitive for demonstrating cerebral lesions.

explanation:
Drug-induced eosinophilic pneumonia—
nitrofurantoin, sulfonamides, penicillin, chlorpropamide, thiazides, tricyclic
antidepressants, hydralazine, mephenesin, mecamylamine, nickel, gold salts,
INH, PAS. Pharmacologic stimuli of asthma—induction of acute episodes with
aspirin, tetrazine, β-adrenergic antagonists, sulfating agents, and
acetylcysteine. Aspirin-sensitive respiratory syndrome primarily affects
adults, usually begins with perineal vasomotor rhinitis that is followed by a

between aspirin and other NSAIDs (indomethacin, fenoprofen, naproxen,
zomepirac sodium, ibuprofen, mefenamic acid, and phenylbutazone).
• Drugs causing pleural effusions and pleural thickening—hydralazine,
INH, methysergide.
• Interstitial pneumonitis/fibrosis (CMDT, 44th Ed., Pg. 298) bleomycin,
nitrofurantoin, busulfan, cyclophosphamide, methysergide, phenytoin
[Davidson: pulmonary fibrosis—bleomycin, methotrexate, amiodarone,
nitrofurantoin].
• Pulmonary infiltration without eosinophilia—amitriptyline,
azathioprine, amiodarone.

question: Amyloidosis of kidney may be seen in the following except
explanation:
Secondary (reactive) systemic amyloidosis:
• Protein type—AA type
• Organs commonly involved—liver, spleen, kidney, adrenals.
• Occurs in chronic infections (suppurative), tuberculosis, bronchiectasis,
chronic osteomyelitis, chronic pyelonephritis, leprosy, autoimmune
disorders (RA, dermatomyositis, scleroderma), inflammatory bowel
disease (ulcerative colitis and Crohn's disease), some tumors (renal cell
carcinoma, Hodgkin's disease).

question: The most specific marker of renal function is

Forty eight hours after sustaining a blunt abdominal injury, a 15-year-old  boy presents with hematuria and pain in the left side of abdomen. On  examination, he has a pulse rate of 96/minute with a BP of 110/70 mm Hg.  His Hb is 10.8 g% with a PCV of 31%. The blood urea is 32 mg%. Abdominal  examination revealed tenderness in left lumbar region but no palpable mass.  The most appropriate investigation to diagnose and find the extent of renal  injury would be
explanation:
Abdominal blunt trauma: -> No blood at meatus ->
Normal -> May pass Foley's catheter. -> Hematuria -> Gross hematuria -> CT
(or IVP) and cystogram. -> No hematuria (dipstick +ve or >5 RBC/HPF) -> With
shock -> CT (or IVP) (and cystogram with pelvic fracture and gross hematuria).
-> Blood at meatus -> Unable to pass catheter -> RUG (retrograde
urethrogram) -> Urology consult.

question: In a young patient, transverse fracture of patella is best treated by

options.c: Strict bed rest with  quadriceps exercises
options.d: Tension band wiring followed by POP cast

explanation:
Treatment of fracture of the patella:
1. Undisplaced fracture—plaster (cylinder) cast extending from groin to
above malleoli with full knee extension for 3 weeks, followed by
physiotherapy.
2. Clean break with separation of fragments (two part fracture).
o Operation consists of reduction of fragments, fixing with tension-
band wiring (TBW), and repair of extensor retinaculum.
o Patellectomy excises the fragments if it is not possible to achieve
accurate reduction of the fragments and repairs extensor
retinaculae.
o Partial patellectomy—in case where one of the fragments
constitute only one of the poles of patella, it is excised. The
major fragment is preserved and extensor retinaculae repaired,
such operation followed by 4-6 weeks cyclinder cast.
3. Comminuted fracture with displacement—best to excise the patella,
irrespective of age.

question: The most common histological type seen in bronchogenic carcinoma is
explanation:
• Adenocarcinoma- most common histological type, common histological
type in young patients and in females, metastases to opposite lung,
peripheral distribution.
• Squamous cell carcinoma- most common histological type in India,
cavitate, central distribution (pancoast tumor is peripheral tumor of
squamous variety), hypercalcemia due to PTH, best prognosis.
• Small cell lung cancers- known as oat cell cancers because of the
packed nature of small dense cells. They are a type of neuroendocrine
tumour, having early metastasis. The pattern of disease, the prognosis
and the results of treatment for small cell (also known as oat cell)
carcinoma differ from all other types sufficiently for these to be
managed differently on the basis of the histological classification.
• C/F- Clinical features depend on: the site of the lesion, invasion of
neighbouring structures, extent of metastases. Hemoptysis is present
in<50%, cough is most common non specific complain. Cigarette
smoking is major risk factor for lung carcinoma and accounts for 85-
95% of all cases. To a lesser extent, atmospheric pollution and certain
occupations (mining of radioactive ore and chromium) contribute.

question: Testicular tumor most sensitive to radiation is
The most accepted modality of treatment of -2D myopia in a 13-year-old  girl is
explanation:
Simple myopia is the most common type of myopia
which progresses during childhood and adolescence and seldom exceeds 5-
6D. It generally stops by the age of 21. Treatment of myopia:
• Concave spherical glass
• Contact lens—ideal substitute in high myopia
• Surgery—(i) radial keratotomy (RK)—for low degree myopia only (–2 to
–6 D), (ii) LASIK—for correcting myopia of –6 to –30 D, (iii)
photorefractive keratectomy—good result low degree (–2 to –6 D)
myopia, (iv) automated lamellar keratectomy (ALK) can correct myopia
of –6 to –32 D.

question: The most common cause of vocal cord palsy is
explanation:
Vocal fold palsy—unilateral left vocal fold palsy is
the most common as a consequence of the long intrathoracic course of left
recurrent laryngeal nerve which arches around the aorta and may be
commonly involved in inflammatory and neoplastic conditions involving the
left hilum. Left vocal cord palsy should be considered to be due to a
carcinoma of lung until proved otherwise. Bilateral recurrent laryngeal nerve
palsy is an occasional and very serious complication of thyroidectomy as
acute dyspnea occurs as a result of paramedian position of both vocal folds
which reduce the airway to 2–3 mm and get sucked during inspiration. Right
nerve more commonly involved in thyroid surgery, while passing through
inferior thyroid vessels (S.K.De).

question: Earliest sign seen in acoustic neuroma is

options.b: Explanation: Acoustic neuroma—Schwannoma of vestibular  division of VIIIth nerve is most common followed by meningiomas. It  comprises 80% of all cerebellopontine angle tumors.  • Clinical features—early acoustic phase—the early symptoms are  unilateral sensorineural hearing loss or unilateral tinnitus or both.  o In advanced case—5th nerve involvement is earliest, pain and  tingling along trigeminal nerve, diminished corneal reflex.  • Diagnosis—MRI investigation of choice, recruitment test is negative,  BERA—most modern diagnostic test.

explanation:
Acoustic neuroma—Schwannoma of vestibular
division of VIIIth nerve is most common followed by meningiomas. It
comprises 80% of all cerebellopontine angle tumors.
• Clinical features—early acoustic phase—the early symptoms are
unilateral sensorineural hearing loss or unilateral tinnitus or both.
o In advanced case—5th nerve involvement is earliest, pain and
tingling along trigeminal nerve, diminished corneal reflex.
• Diagnosis—MRI investigation of choice, recruitment test is negative,
BERA—most modern diagnostic test.

question: Reversal of nondepolarizing muscle relaxant's action is done with the  help of
explanation:
Common antagonists to nondepolarizing relaxants:
• Neostigmine—anticholinesterase drug given IV, very slowly to
counteract the action of nondepolarizing muscle relaxants (0.5–2.5
mg). In order to counteract adverse effects (excessive salivary and
bronchial secretion, bronchospasm, and contraction of gut) atropine
should always be given, at least 5 minutes before neostigmine.
• Edrophonium—
o Short-acting synthetic anticholinesterase
o Its anticurare action is due to its depolarizing action along with
its direct action on motor end plate.
o Atropine should be administered beforehand to combat
muscarinic effects.

question: Which one of following is not a perceptual abnormality?
explanation:
• Hallucination—a perception that occurs in the absence of a stimulus.
• Illusion—a misinterpretation of stimuli arising from external object(s).
• Delusion—a false, unshakable belief which is not amenable to
reasoning and is not in keeping with the patients socio-culture and
educational background.

options.b: Explanation: Urethral carbuncle—common in elderly women. Urethral carbuncle—common in elderly women.
Presents as soft, raspberry like, highly vascular, pea size pedunculated
granulomatous mass at posterior urethral wall near external meatus. Clinical
features—frequency of micturition and pain and occasionally bleeding.
Treatment is by excision and diathermy coagulation of the base of the stalk.
Antibiotics to treat underlying chronic urethritis. Excision biopsy is the

explanation:
Sinus bradycardia—a sinus rate less than 60 per
minute may occur in normal people during sleep and is a common finding in
athletes. Some pathological causes are MI, sick sinus syndrome, hypothermia,
hypothyroidism, cholestatic jaundice, raised intracranial pressure, drugs (β-
adrenoceptor antagonist, digoxin, and verapamil). Acute symptomatic sinus
bradycardia usually responds to IV atropine 0.6 mg.

question: Which one of the following statements is true of Stoke-Adams attack?

options.a: It is usually preceded by an aura
options.b: Focal neurological signs are commonly  observed during the attack
options.c: Explanation: Adams-Stoke attacks—episodes of ventricular  asystole may complicate complete heart block or Mobitz type II second  degree AV block and can occur in patients with sinoatrial disease. This may  cause recurrent syncope or Adams-Stoke attacks. Episode is characterized by  sudden loss of consciousness without warning. Convulsion due to cerebral  ischemia in prolonged asystole. In contrast to epilepsy, recovery is rapid.
options.d: It is caused by recurrent paroxysmal  tachyarrhythmias

explanation:
Adams-Stoke attacks—episodes of ventricular
asystole may complicate complete heart block or Mobitz type II second
degree AV block and can occur in patients with sinoatrial disease. This may
cause recurrent syncope or Adams-Stoke attacks. Episode is characterized by
sudden loss of consciousness without warning. Convulsion due to cerebral
ischemia in prolonged asystole. In contrast to epilepsy, recovery is rapid.

question: Which one of the following is an absolute contraindication to the use of  thrombolytic agent in the setting of an acute anterior wall myocardial  infarction?

options.a: History of CVA with hemiparesis one month ago
options.d: Patient is on warfarin for AF with INR  ration 1.8

explanation:
Contraindication of fibrinolytic agents:
1. Clear contraindication—(i) history of cerebrovascular hemorrhage at
any time, (ii) nonhemorrhagic stroke or other cerebrovascular event
within the past year, (iii) marked hypertension (systolic >180 mmHg
and/or >110 mmHg) at any time during acute presentation, (iv)
suspicion of aortic dissection, (v) active internal bleeding (excluding
menses).
2. Relative contraindications—(i) current use of anticoagulants (INR ≥2),
(ii) a recent (<2 weeks) invasive or surgical procedure, (iii) prolonged
(>10 minutes) CPR, (iv) known bleeding diathesis, (v) pregnancy, (vi)
hemorrhagic ophthalmic conditions (e.g., hemorrhagic diabetic
retinopathy), (vii) active peptic ulcer, (viii) history of severe
hypertension that is currently adequately controlled.

question: Interstitial pulmonary fibrosis is associated with the following, except
What is the most common presentation of sarcoidosis?
explanation:
Presentation of sarcoidosis:
• Asymptomatic—abnormal routine chest radiograph (30%) (bilateral
hilar lymphadenopathy) [Davidson]
• Respiratory and constitutional symptoms (20–30%)
• Erythema nodosum and arthralgia (20–30%)
• Ocular symptoms (5–10%)
• Skin sarcoidosis (5%)
• Other (1%), e.g., hypercalcemia, DI. Harrison—lung is almost always
involved, most patients have symptoms referable to respiratory
system. Kumar and Clark—sarcoidosis is a multisystem granulomatous
disorder, commonly affecting young adults and usually presenting with
bilateral hilar lymphadenopathy, pulmonary infiltration, and skin and
eye lesions. The most common presentation is with respiratory
symptoms and abnormalities found in chest X-ray (50%). There is
confusion between answer "d" and "b" in different books mentioned
above. Now, its upon you to choose the answer.

explanation:
Causes of chronic diarrhea:
1. Osmotic diarrhea—clues—stool volume decreases with fasting,
increased stool osmotic gap; (i) Medications—antacids, lactulose,
sorbitol, (ii) disaccharidase deficiency—lactose intolerance, (iii)
factitious diarrhea—magnesium (antacids, laxatives).
2. Secretory diarrhea—clues—large volume (>1 L/day), little change with
fasting, normal stool osmotic gap; (i) hormonally mediated—vipoma,
carcinoid, medullary carcinoma of thyroid (calcitonin), Zollinger-Ellison
syndrome (gastrin), (ii) factitious diarrhea (laxative abuse),
phenolphthalein, cascara, senna, (iii) villous adenoma, (iv) bile salt
malabsorption (ileal resection, Crohn's ileitis, postcholecystectomy, (v)
medications.

question: Renal osteodystrophy which accompanies chronic renal failure results  from

options.c: Loss of vitamin D and calcium via  dialysis
options.d: Impaired renal production of 1,25-dihydroxy vitamin D3

explanation:
Renal (azotemic) osteodystrophy—the metabolic
bone disease which accompanies CRF consists of mixture of osteomalacia,
hyperparathyroid bone disease (osteitis fibrosa), osteoporosis, and
osteosclerosis.
• Osteomalacia results from diminished activity of the renal 1-α
hydroxylase enzyme, with failure to convert cholecalciferol to its active
metabolite 1, 25(OH)2 cholecalciferol.
• Deficiency of 1, 25(OH)2 cholecalciferol leads to diminished intestinal
absorption of calcium, hypocalcemia, and reduction in calcification of
osteoid.
• Osteitis fibrosa results from secondary hyperparathyroidism.
• Osteosclerosis—in sacral area, at the base of the skull and vertebral.
Cause is not known. Laboratory investigations—hypocalcemia,
hyperphosphatemia, raised alkaline phosphatase, and parathyroid
hormone.

explanation:

question: Tuberculoid leprosy has following features except
explanation:
Distinguishing features of various types of leprosy:
• Tuberculoid (TT):
o Lesion: Hypopigmented, clearly demarcated Macule papule
o No. of lesions: Usually single
o Sensation: Unlike other part of body, patch on face is not
anesthetic
o Peripheral nerve involvement: Markedly thickened, usually single
o AFB in lesion: Absent
o Lepromin test: +++
• Borderline Tuberculoid (BT):
o Lesion: Hypopigmented, macules/papule, more numerous,
smaller, "satellite lesion" less distinct border
o No. of lesions: Usually more numerous
o Sensation: ↓↓ to ↓↓↓
o Peripheral nerve involvement: Common, usually single
o AFB in lesion: ±
o Lepromin test: +/++
• Borderline Borderline (BB):
o Lesion: More lesion than BT, orders more vague
o No. of lesions: Numerous
o Sensation: ↓ to ↓↓
o Peripheral nerve involvement: Common, more than one
o AFB in lesion: +
o Lepromin test: -
• Borderline Lepromatous (BL):
o Lesion: Similar to BB
o No. of lesions: Many
o Sensation: ↓
o Peripheral nerve involvement: Severe, asymmetrical
o AFB in lesion: ++
o Lepromin test: -
• Lepromatous (LL):
o Lesion: Macular/papular symmetrically distributed
o No. of lesions: Numerous
o Sensation: Lost only in later stages
o Peripheral nerve involvement: None till late
o AFB in lesion: +++
o Lepromin test: -

Relatively resistant: squamous cell carcinoma, carcinoma cervix,  bladder and rectum, hypernephroma.  4. Highly resistant: melanoma, osteosarcoma, pancreatic carcinoma.

explanation:

Wilms' tumor.
2. Moderately sensitive: small cell lung carcinoma, breast cancer, basal

question: Match list I (diuretics) with list II (site of action in the nephron) and  select the correct answer using the codes given below the lists:  List I (Diuretics) A. Acetazolamide B. Furosemide C. Hydrochlorothiazide D.  Triamterene  List II (Site of action in the nephron)  1. Loop of Henle  2. Proximal convoluted tubule  3. Distal convoluted tubule  4. Collecting duct  Code:
explanation:
Sites of action, mechanism, and other important facts of
diuretics:
• Loop or high ceiling (furosemide, ethacrynic acid):
o Main site of action: Thick ascending limb of loop of Henle
o Mechanism of action: Inhibition of Na+, K+, 2Cl- carrier system
[20–30% sodium excretion in urine]
o Other features: ↓K+, ↓Mg2+, ↓Ca2+. Metabolic hypochloremic
alkalosis, hyperuricemia except indacrinone and ticrynafen.
• Thiazide:
o Main site of action: Distal portion of ascending limb of loop of
Henle (cortical dilating system) and early portion of distal
convoluted tubule
o Mechanism of action: Inhibition of Na+Cl- Co-transport system
[5-10% sodium excretion in urine]
o Other features: ↓K+, ↓Mg2+, ↑Ca2+. Metabolic hyponatremic,
hypochloremic alkalosis, hyperuricemia.
• K-sparing diuretics (Spironolactone):
o Main site of action: Distal collecting tubule and duct
o Mechanism of action: [<5% Na excretion in urine] direct
antagonism aldosterone receptors
o Other features: Gynecomastia.
• Triamterene, Amiloride:
o Mechanism of action: Inhibition of active Na+ absorption in
collecting tubule
o Other features: ARF when given with indomethacin.
• Carbonic anhydrase inhibitors (acetazolamide, ethoxzolamide, etc.):
o Main site of action: Proximal and distal convoluted tubule
o Mechanism of action: (CA) CO2 + H2O <---> H2CO3 <-> H+ +
HCO3-, H+ not available for H+/Na+ exchange, leading to
excretion of Na+ accompanied by water.
o Other features: Increased urinary amount of Na+, K+ and HCO3-
resulting Alkaline urine.

A 60-year-old woman  presents with adnexal mass and nodules in the pouch of Douglas. The most  likely diagnosis is
explanation:
Differentiation between
benign and malignant ovarian tumors Benign: • Clinical examination:
Unilateral, mobile, cystic, surface—smooth; Ascites—absent; Growth—slow;
Patient's age—younger; Nodules in POD—absent. • Laparotomy findings:
Ascites—absent; Adhesions—absent; Exophytic growth on surface—absent;
Cut section—cystic; Peritoneal nodules—absent. • Ultrasonography: Cystic
areas with uniformly thin and thick multiple linear echoes. Malignant: • Clinical
examination: Bilateral, fixed solid/variegated, irregular surface; Ascites—
present; Growth—rapid; Patient's age—older (menopausal, postmenopausal);
Nodules in pouch of Douglas—present (metastatic). • Laparotomy findings:
Ascites—present, often hemorrhagic; Adhesions—Present; Exophytic growth
on surface—Present; Cut section—Solid and hemorrhagic; Peritoneal
nodules—present. • Ultrasonography: Cystic areas with irregular
heterogeneous solid part in >50% of total tumor volume. • Ovarian malignancy
with metastatic deposits in pouch of Douglas can be mistaken for
endometriosis. Endometriosis is a disease of the childbearing period.

question: Bluish suburethral nodule  seen in lower one-third of vagina is suggestive of
explanation:
Choriocarcinoma: The majority of
primary growths arise in the body of the uterus and develop first within the
endometrial cavity. Choriocarcinoma always infiltrates the wall of the uterus.
Metastasis early and disseminated; usually occurs by the way of bloodstream.
Metastases which can be detected easily are those found in lower third of
vagina and at vulva. Such metastases form purple hemorrhagic projections
either into the vagina or around the vaginal orifice. Their appearance is
characteristic and pathognomonic. On examination, a vaginal metastasis
appears as bluish-red vascular tumor which bleeds easily on touch.

was held in March 2001. The first regular census in India was taken in 1881 and
thereafter in 10 years' interval. Sample registration system (SRS)—in 1960, to
provide estimates of birth and death rates, is a dual system consisting
continuous enumeration and an independent survey every 6 months.

question: In which one of the following are  pseudocarriers found?
explanation:
Pseudocarriers—carriers of
avirulent organisms are called pseudocarriers and are not important
epidemiologically. Carriers:
1. Type a. Incubatory—measles, mumps, polio, pertussis, influenza,
diphtheria, hepatitis B (serum hepatitis). b. Convalescent—typhoid fever,
dysentery (amoebic and bacillary), cholera, diphtheria, whooping cough.
c. Healthy carriers—poliomyelitis, cholera, meningococcal meningitis,
salmonellosis, and diphtheria.
2. By duration a. Temporary—includes incubatory, convalescent, and
healthy carrier. b. Chronic carriers—typhoid fever, hepatitis B, dysentery,
cerebrospinal meningitis, malaria, gonorrhea, in cholera chronic carrier
states occur infrequently with longest carrier state to >10 years.
