question: In the context of health education,  what is the correct sequence of stages people generally pass through before  they adopt a new idea and practice?
explanation:
Adoption of new ideas and practice: (i) awareness, (ii) interest, (iii) evaluation,
(iv) trial, (v) adoption. The above stages are not necessarily rigid; there may be
skipping of stages.

question: Match list I (disease) with list II (hereditary  trait) and select the correct answer using the codes given below the lists: List  I (Disease) A. Polycystic kidney B. Cystic fibrosis C. Retinitis pigmentosa D.  Familial hypophosphatemia List II (Hereditary trait)  1. Autosomal recessive  2. X-linked dominant  3. Autosomal dominant  4. X-linked recessive
What is the maximum permissible  level of occupational exposure to ionizing radiation per year to the whole  body of an individual as set by the International Commission of Radiological  Protection?
explanation:
The International Commission on Radiological
Protection (ICRP) recommended that genetic dose to the whole population
from all sources additional to the natural background radiation should not
exceed 5 rem over a period of 30 years. • 1 becquerel (Bq) is equal to 1
disintegration/second. 1 Bq = 27 picocurie. • Roentgen is the unit of exposure.
Amount of radiation exposed in air at a given point. 1 roentgen = 2.58 × 10⁴
coulomb/kg. • Rad is the unit of absorbed dose, i.e., amount of radioactive
energy absorbed per gram of tissue. 1 rad = 0.01 Gy (gray). • Rem is the
product of absorbed dose and modifying factor, 100 rem = 1 Sv (Sievert).

question: Elimination of neonatal tetanus is said to  be achieved when the cases per 1,000 births are
explanation:
Classification of district (neonatal tetanus
elimination) • NT high risk: Incidence rate (live birth) >1/1000 | Attended
deliveries OR <50% | Immunization coverage (TT) OR <70% • NT control:
Incidence rate (live birth) <1/1000 | Attended deliveries AND >50% |
Immunization coverage (TT) AND >70% • NT elimination: Incidence rate (live
birth) <0.1/1000 | Attended deliveries AND >75% | Immunization coverage (TT)
AND >90%

question: Consider the following radiological  features:  1. Diaphyseal fracture  2. Ground-glass appearance  3. Rarefied zone at metaphysis  4. Subperiosteal hemorrhage Which of the above is/are seen in scurvy?
Which one of the following  IV fluids is the best for treating a severe case of cholera?
explanation:
Intravenous rehydration—only
for the initial rehydration of severely dehydrated patients who are in shock or
unable to drink. Solution for intravenous infusion (WHO recommendation):
1. Ringer lactate solution (Hartmann's solution)—best available solution
2. Diarrhea treatment solution (DTS)
3. Normal saline—if nothing else is available. Dose—100 mL/kg; first—30
mL/kg in 5 hours (infant) and 2½ hours (older).

question: Which one of the following conditions is  not considered as acute flaccid paralysis (AFP) under Polio Surveillance  Programme?
explanation:
D/D of acute
flaccid paralysis—poliomyelitis, Guillain-Barre (GB) syndrome, transverse
myelitis, traumatic neuritis. D/D of poliomyelitis—infective polyneuritis (GB
syndrome), severe hypokalemia, postdiphtheritic paralysis, transverse myelitis,
botulism, pseudoparalysis.

question: Which one of the following is not  associated with childhood obesity?
explanation:
Causes of childhood obesity:
1. Congenital—environment factor (>95% of all cases)
2. Pathological: • Endocrine—GH insufficiency, hypopituitarism, hyper- or
hypogonadotropic hypogonadism, hypothyroidism,
pseudohypoparathyroidism, Cushing's syndrome, hyperandrogenic ovary
syndrome, craniopharyngioma. • Hypothalamic—head injury, infection,
brain tumor, radiation, postneurosurgery, Fröhlich syndrome (obesity,
short stature, pubertal failure, hyperphagia, and sometimes blindness). •
Drugs—antiepileptic, steroid, estrogen. • Genetic syndrome—Prader-
Willi syndrome (hyperphagia, obesity, mental retardation,
hypogonadism, cryptorchidism, deletion on short arm), Laurence-Moon-
Biedl-Bardet syndrome (obesity, hypogenitalism, short stature, retinitis
pigmentosa, renal anomalies, polydactyly, syndactyly, mental
retardation, autosomal recessive), Beckwith-Wiedemann, Carpenter
syndrome. • Monogenic disorder—Leptin deficiency, Leptin resistance,
etc.

question: Which one of the following  problems is usually not seen in a preterm baby of 28 weeks gestation?
explanation:
Problem of low-birth-
weight (LBW) neonates Preterm: • Birth asphyxia • Hypothermia,
hypoglycemia, hypocalcemia • Infections • Hyperbilirubinemia (more severe) •
Feeding difficulties, hyaline membrane disease (respiratory distress syndrome)
• Apneic spells, intravascular hemorrhage, necrotizing enterocolitis, metabolic
acidosis, retinopathy of prematurity Small for dates: • Birth asphyxia,
bronchopulmonary dysplasia, microcoagulation leading to DIC • Hypothermia •
Infections • Hyperbilirubinemia • Meconium aspiration syndrome (MAS) •
Hypoglycemia, polycythemia, anemia, thrombocytopenia, pulmonary
hemorrhage, electrolyte abnormality (hyperphosphatemia, hypokalemia) MAS
usually occurs in postmature and small for date babies.

question: The most common organism  responsible for acute infective endocarditis in children having an underlying  heart disease is
explanation:
Organisms causing
endocarditis:
1. Native-valve endocarditis: a. Community acquired—Staphylococcus
aureus (most common), streptococci b. Nosocomial—S. aureus (most
common), enterococci
2. Prosthetic valve endocarditis at indicated time of onset (months) after
valve surgery: a. <2 months—coagulase-negative staphylococci (most
common), S. aureus b. 2–12 months—coagulase-negative
Staphylococcus (most common), S.aureus, and enterococci c. >12
months—streptococci (most common), S. aureus
3. Endocarditis in injection drug users: a. Right sided—S. aureus b. Left
sided—enterococci, S. aureus c. Total—S. aureus.

question: Consider the following:  1. Small for date baby  2. Large for date baby  3. Erythroblastosis fetalis  4. Birth asphyxia Which of these clinical settings predispose(s) to neonatal  hypoglycemia?
explanation:
Causes of neonatal hypoglycemia •
Common: Small for dates, preterm, infants of diabetic mothers, polycythemia,
septicemia, hypothermia, birth asphyxia, respiratory distress, and Rh
incompatibility (erythroblastosis) • Rare: Hyperinsulin states—beta-cell
hyperplasia (nesidioblastosis), adenoma of beta-cells. Deficiency of
hormones—glucagon, hGH, epinephrine, ACTH. Metabolic disease—
galactosemia, glycogen storage disease, fructosemia, Maple syrup urine
disease.

question: Which congenital metabolic disorder is  associated with convulsions, cataract, and mental retardation?
Normocytic normochromic anemia  may be the feature of the following conditions, except

options.b: Anemia due to chronic blood loss

explanation:
Morphologic
classification of anemia • Microcytic hypochromic: MCV, MCH, MCHC—all
reduced. Causes: 1. Iron-deficiency anemia (a. During infancy—nutritional,
posthemorrhagic, b. Older child—posthemorrhagic, nutritional), 2. Ineffective
erythropoiesis—thalassemia, pyridoxine responsive anemia, lead poisoning,
dyserythropoietic anemia. • Normocytic, normochromic: MCV, MCH, MCHC—
all normal. Causes: 1. Acute blood loss, 2. Hemolysis (reticulocyte count high),
3. Impaired cell production (reticulocyte count low) - a. Normal leucocyte and
platelet: physiological anemia of infancy, infections, pure red cell aplasia. b.
Normal or decreased leucocyte and platelets: chronic renal and liver disease,
hypothyroidism. c. Reduced leucocyte and platelet: aplastic anemia, leukemia.
• Macrocytic: MCV—raised. Causes: 1. Megaloblastic erythropoiesis - a.
Nutritional—vitamin B12 deficiency, folate deficiency, Kwashiorkor. b. Drugs—
methotrexate, phenytoin. 2. Nonmegaloblastic erythropoiesis—chronic
hemolytic anemia, liver disease, hypothyroidism, Diamond–Blackfan syndrome.
Malabsorption.

question: Consider the following statements:  Riboflavin deficiency may cause  1. Glossitis  2. Peripheral neuropathy  3. Nasolabial dyssebacia  4. Circumcorneal vascularization Which of the statements given above  is/are correct?

In which of the following disorders  is penicillin not used for antimicrobial prophylaxis?
explanation:
Prophylactic uses of penicillins:
1. Rheumatic fever—most convenient regimen—benzathine penicillin, 1.2
MU every 4 weeks till 18 years of age or 5 years after attack, whichever is
more convenient.
2. Gonorrhea or syphilis—procaine penicillin or benzathine penicillin, 2.4
MU single dose before or within 12 hours of contact.
3. Bacterial endocarditis.
4. Agranulocytosis patients.
5. Surgical infections—procaine penicillin, 1 MU with aminoglycosides
injected IM 1 hour before and 8–12 hours after surgery.
Chemoprophylaxis of close contacts with confirmed meningococcal
disease—rifampicin (DOC unless the organism is known to be sensitive to
sulfadiazine) 600 mg twice daily for 2 days.

question: Which one of the following  antiviral drugs is helpful in children having severe bronchiolitis with  associated congenital heart disease?
Which one of the following intrauterine  devices is used in women with menorrhagia?
Which one of the following events is  associated with ovulation?
explanation:
• Proliferative phase: 7–14
days. Epithelial cells are columnar with their nuclei situated basally but show
no evidence of secretory activity. Postmenstrually, the endometrium is only 1
mm thick, whereas at the end of proliferative phase, it is 2–3 mm. [shaw—3.5
mm]. • Secretory phase: 15th day and persists until onset of menstruation. The
characteristic subnuclear vacuolation (spherical translucent areas as globe
between nuclei and basement membrane of epithelial cells) is presumptive
evidence of progesterone activity and therefore of ovulation. The glands
become crenated and assume a characteristic "cork screw shaped" and finally
"saw toothed." In secretory phase, the maximum thickness is 5–7 mm. [shaw—
8–10 mm]. LH surge: The onset of the LH surge, which occurs 28–32 hours
before ovulation, is the most reliable indicator of the timing of ovulation. The
estrogen peak occurs about 24–36 hours before ovulation, the LH peak is about
10–12 hours before ovulation, and small FSH rise occurs along with LH surge.
LH peak persists for about 24 hours. LH surge stimulates completion of
reduction division of the oocyte and initiates leutinization of the granulosa
cells, synthesis of progesterone and prostaglandins. Method of detecting
ovulation—basal body temperature (BBT), endometrial biopsy, LH test, and
molimina, elevated blood progesterone.

Consider the following statements with  regard to the MTP Act:  1. MTP can be performed by a doctor who has performed at least 25 MTP  under supervision and independently.  2. Place for MTP must be approved by the government.  3. Facility for doing emergency laparotomy should be available. Which of  the statements given above are correct?
explanation:
The Medical Termination of Pregnancy
(MTP) Act was passed by parliament in 1971 and came into force on April 1,
1972. The MTP Act, 1971 lays down:
1. The conditions and grounds on which pregnancy may be terminated.
2. The persons who are permitted to perform this procedure.
3. The place where such terminations can be performed. i. The conditions
under which pregnancy can be terminated: a. Medical—in which
continuation of pregnancy: (i) endangers the mother's life, (ii) causes
grave injury to her physical or mental health. b. Eugenic—in which there
is substantial risk of child being born with serious handicaps due to
physical or mental abnormalities. c. Humanitarian—when pregnancy
caused by rape or incest. d. Socioeconomic—when actual or reasonably
foreseeable environments (whether social or economic) could lead to
risk of injury to the health of mother. e. Failure of contraceptive devices
or methods—written consent of guardian—(i)women <18 years, (ii) in
lunatic >18 years. ii. The person or persons can perform MTP: a. When
the registered medical practitioner has assisted in at least 25 MTPs in an
authorized center and having certificate. b. Has got 6 months house
surgeon training in obstetrics and gynecology. c. One has got diploma or
degree in obstetrics and gynecology. Termination is permitted up to 20
weeks. When the pregnancy exceeds 12 weeks, opinion of two
registered medical practitioners is necessary. iii. Where abortion is
performed: In hospitals established or maintained by government or a
place approved for the purpose of this Act by government.
4. Pregnancy can only be terminated on the written consent of women.
Husband consent not required. Pregnancy in minor girl or lunatic cannot
be terminated without written consent of parent and guardian. Under
new rules (1975), nongovernment institutions obtain a license from the
Chief Medical Officer of district.

question: Which one among the following is the  safest method for midtrimester abortion?
Mifepristone (RU-486) used for  medical abortion is
explanation:
RU-486 (mifepristone): Recently
developed 19-norsteroid with potent competitive antiprogestational and
significant antiglucocorticoid as well as antiandrogenic activity. Uses:
1. Termination of pregnancy—up to 7 weeks. 600 mg single oral dose (60–
85% complete abortion) up to 48 hours later 400 mg oral misoprostol
(>90% success rate)
2. Postcoital contraceptive—600 mg within 72 hours
3. Cervical ripening
4. Once a month contraceptive
5. Induction of labor
6. Cushing's syndrome. Other recently developed antiprogestins—
onapristone (pure antagonist) and gestrinone (more efficacious in
endometriosis). [Shaw—Oral mifepristone (200 mg) followed by 36–48
hours later by 600 µg of vaginal mifepristone and then 400 µg of oral or
vaginal mifepristone every 3 hours with maximum 5 doses.]

question: Match list I (nutrient) with list II (benefit)  and select the correct answer using the codes given below the lists: List I  (Nutrient) A. Vitamin D B. Folic acid C. Calcium List II (Benefit)  1. Decreases neural tube defects  2. Decreases pre-eclampsia  3. Used for osteoporosis
explanation:
• Folic acid (4 mg/day), starting 4 weeks

Except for calcium supplementation and aspirin, none of the others have stood
the test of time.

Japanese encephalitis  4. Food poisoning
explanation:
Epizootic: An outbreak (epidemic)
of disease in animal population, e.g., anthrax, brucellosis, rabies, influenza, Rift

question: Selective screening refers to screening tests  applied to a
explanation:
Types of screening:
1. Mass screening—offered to all, irrespective of particular risk individuals
may run of contracting the disease in question. When a number of mass
screening procedure were subjected to critical review, there appeared to
be little justification for their use. Indiscriminate mass screening is
therefore little is not a useful preventive measure.
2. Selective or high-risk screening—screening will be more productive if
applied selectively to high-risk groups.
3. Multiphasic screening—application of two or more screening tests in
combination to a large number of people at one time than to carry out
separate screening tests for single diseases. It has not shown any benefit
accruing to the population in terms of mortality and morbidity reduction.
Its utility is doubtful.

question: The "specificity" of a screening test is its  ability to correctly identify high proportion of
Which one of the following is not the  activity under Revised National Tuberculosis Control Programme?

options.a: Short-course chemotherapy provided free of charge

question: If the size of the random sample is  increased, we would expect

options.b: The standard error of the mean to decrease

explanation:
Standard
error of mean (Sex)—SD/√n, standard deviation (SD) of sample divided by the
square root of the number of observations in the sample. Its value varies
directly with the size of SD. Greater the SD, greater will be the SE as will happen
in small samples. Sampling error or chance variation has to be minimized by
reducing the SD which can be done only by taking a large sample.

question: Match list I (situations) with list II (rates  which most closely relate) and select the correct answer using the codes  given below the lists: List I (Situations) A. When it is used as an incidence rate  B. When it is an actual observed rate for the entire population C. When it is  expressed as a standardized ratio D. When a standard population is borrowed  List II (Rates which most closely relate)  1. Direct age-adjusted rate in epidemic investigation  2. Indirect age-adjusted rate  3. Attack rate  4. Crude birth rate
explanation:
• Attack rate: An incidence rate
(usually expressed as percentage), used only when the population is exposed
to risk for a limited period of time as during an epidemic. • Direct
standardization: In the first place, a "standard population" is selected. A
standard population is one for which the numbers in each age and sex group
are known. The next step is to apply to the standard population, the age-
specific rates of the population whose crude death rate is to be adjusted or
standardized. As a result, for each age group, an "expected" number of deaths
in standard population is obtained, these are added together for all the age
groups to give total expected deaths. The final operation is to divide the
"expected" total number of deaths by the total of standard population, which
yields the standardized or age-adjusted rate. • Indirect age standardization: The
simplest and most useful form of indirect standardization is the standardized
mortality ratio (SMR); SMR = Observed death/Expected death × 100.

question: Most important "significance test" to find  out the strength of association between variables is

options.a: Standard error of difference of mean

explanation:
Chi-squared test is applied
to find association between two events occurring together when there are
more than two classes or more than two samples.
1. Appropriate when both predictor and outcome variables are
dichotomous.
2. As comparison of prevalence of HBsAg in medical and dental students is
classified by presence or absence of antigen and by medical or dental
students' status.

question: A coefficient of correlation value of r =  +0.8 indicates

options.a: Strong positive relationship between two variables
options.b: A strong negative  relationship between two variables
options.c: An insignificant association between  two variables
options.d: One variable is the cause of the other variable

explanation:
Coefficient of correlation is a measure of extent or degree of relationship
between two variables (x or y) represented by symbol "r." Types of correlation:
1. Perfect negative correlation—if r is +1, it indicates strong positive
association, e.g., not found in nature (but approaching to that extent are
height and weight, age and weight).
2. Perfect negative correlation—a value near –1 indicates strong negative
association, e.g., not seen in nature (approaching to that extent are
mean weekly temperature and number of colds in winter).
3. Moderately positive correlation—if r lies between 0 and +1, i.e., 0 < r < 1,
e.g., IMR and overcrowding, temperature, and pulse.
4. Moderately negative correlation—if r lies between –1 and 0, i.e., –1< r <
0, e.g., age and vital capacity in adult, income, and IMR.
5. Absolutely no correlation—if r = 0, it indicates no association between x
and y, e.g., height and pulse rate.

question: Age-adjusted death rates are used to

options.a: Correct death rates for errors in the statement of age
options.b: Correct death rates  for missing age information
options.c: Compare death in persons of same age group
options.d: Eliminate the effects of differences in the age distributions of population in  comparing the death rates

explanation:
Adjusted or
standardized rates: The age adjustment or "age standardization" removes the
confounding effect of different age structure and yields a single standardized or
adjusted rate, by which the mortality experience can be compared directly. If
we want to compare the death rates of two populations with different age
composition, the crude death rate is not the right yardstick. This is because
rates are only comparable if the population upon which they are based is
comparable, and it is most cumbersome to use a series of age-specific death
rates.

question: Most essential fatty acid which serves as a  basis for production of other essential fatty acid (EFA) in the body is
explanation:
The most important essential fatty acid
(EFA) is linoleic acid, which serves as a basis for production of other essential

question: Match list I (disease) with list II (vector)  and select the correct answer using the codes given below the lists: List I  (Disease) A. Rickettsial pox B. Trench fever C. Murine typhus D. Rocky mountain  spotted fever List II (Vector)  1. Flea  2. Mite  3. Tick  4. Louse
A 2-year-old healthy child suddenly  develops breathlessness while playing. What is the most likely cause?
explanation:
Foreign body aspiration:
Young children between 1 and 4 years of age are especially prone to aspirate.
The typical history of sudden paroxysm of violent cough, hoarseness, dyspnea,
and cyanosis. The characteristic signs are wheeze and audible slap and palpable
thud produced by momentary expiratory impaction at subglottic level. In 80%,
there will be history of wheezing. On chest radiograph, most often
hyperinflation on the side of foreign body, about 60% on right side.
Ghai/Nelson—right and left main stem bronchial foreign body aspirations occur
with roughly equal frequency in children.

question: Assertion (A): A child who suffered from  poliomyelitis should be immunized with polio vaccine. Reason (R): Viral  infections do not produce sufficient immunity.
explanation:
Immunity following poliovirus
infection is fairly solid although reinfection can occur since infection with one
type does not protect completely against other two types of viruses.

question: Assertion (A): Simultaneous  administration of hepatitis B vaccine and hepatitis B immunoglobulin is more  effective than HBIG alone in prevention of hepatitis B after a recent exposure.  Reason (R): HBIG does not interfere with the antibody response to hepatitis B  vaccine.
Assertion (A): The usefulness of crude  death rate (CDR) as an indicator of mortality is restricted. Reason (R): CDR is  not influenced by the age-sex composition of population.
explanation:
CDR – the number of deaths
(from all causes) per 1,000 estimated midyear population in 1 year in a given
place. The limitation of CDR is exposed when we compare the age-specific rates
between the two populations. CDR has major disadvantages, that is, they lack
comparability for communities with populations that differ by age, sex, and
race. In many countries, CDR is the only available indicator of health when used
for international comparison; the usefulness of CDR is restricted because it is
influenced by age-sex composition of the population.

question: Consider the following statements: A  16-week-old child:  1. Makes polysyllabic vowel sounds  2. Gets excited at sight of food  3. Enjoys mirror  4. Laughs out loud Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
explanation:
• Four-month-old infants described as
"hatching" socially, becoming interested in a wider world. During feeding,
infants no longer focus exclusively on the mother but become distracted. In the
mother's arm, the infant may literally turn around, preferring to face outward.
• Infants at this age also explore their own bodies, starting intently at their
hands, vocalizing, blowing bubbles, and touching their ears, cheeks, and
genitalias. • By 3 months—often vocalizes with two or more different syllables.
• By 6 months—occasionally vocalizes with four or more different syllables at
one time. • By 9 months—intonated babble with sequence of sounds. Babble
more complete with different vowels and consonants.

question: Treatment of choice for thalassemia  major is
explanation:

question: Match list I (condition) with list II (timing  of surgery) and select the correct answer using the codes given below the  lists: List I (Condition) A. Cystic hygroma B. Undescended testes C. Cleft palate  D. Umbilical hernia List II (Timing of surgery)  1. Around 1 year of age  2. Almost never needed  3. 7–9 months of age  4. Soon after diagnosis
explanation:
• Cleft lip: 6–12 weeks • Cleft
palate: 9–12 months • Umbilical hernia: After 1 year, when fails to disappear
(90% case spontaneously cured) • Undescended testes: Between 1 and 2 years
• Hypospadias, chordee correction: 1 year • Urethroplasty: 3 years •
Hirschsprung's disease: 1 year • Congenital hypertrophic pyloric stenosis: 4–6
weeks • Congenital inguinal hernia: As soon as diagnosed • Congenital
hydrocele: After 6 months • PDA: At birth • COA: 3–4 years or soon after infant
has become medically stabilized

question: Consider the following conditions:  1. Mental retardation  2. Stunting of growth  3. Hypotonia  4. Congenital heart disease Which of the above are the features of  Edward's syndrome?

Consider the following statements:  Specific features suggestive of tricuspid regurgitation consist of:  1. Systolic pulsations of the liver  2. Diastolic thrill at left lower sternal border  3. Prominent V waves in jugular venous pulse  4. Deep S waves in V1 lead and tall R waves in V6 lead ECG Which of the  statements given above is/are correct?
explanation:
Tricuspid regurgitation • Specific features: (i)
Prominent "V" wave in jugular venous pressure (JVP), (ii) systolic pulsation of
liver, (iii) systolic murmur at left lower sternal border increasing intensity with
inspiration, (iv) right ventricular 3rd sound or short tricuspid delayed diastolic
murmur increasing during inspiration may be audible in marked TR. • As a rule,
almost all patients with TR also have features of pulmonary artery
hypertension. • ECG shows changes characteristic of enlargement of right
ventricle. [In aortic regurgitation, the ECG shows some increase in left
ventricular voltages with deep "S" wave in V1 and tall "R" wave in V6. There is
also deep "Q" wave in left chest leads accompanied by tall "T" wave. The
pattern of deep "Q" wave and tall "T" wave has been called the "diastolic
overloading pattern" of left ventricle].

question: Which one of the following  statements is not correct?

options.b: Hemoglobin level becomes  lower than the prepregnant level
options.c: Serum level of bilirubin increases in  pregnancy
options.d: Increased frequency of micturition is common during pregnancy

question: A patient is found to  have active tuberculosis diagnosed during the sixth month of pregnancy.  Which one of the following combinations should be recommended?
Diagnosis of acute  fatty liver of pregnancy is usually made by
explanation:
Acute fatty liver of
pregnancy • Histological changes—hepatocytes with increased microvascular
fat, fibrin deposits in hepatic sinusoids. • May occur in late pregnancy and may
be associated with pre-eclampsia. • Laboratory investigations—serum bilirubin
>170 µmol/L (10 mg/dL). • Alanine aminotransferase and aspartate
aminotransferase level—5–8 µkat/L (300–500 U/L). • PT—prolonged. DIC (↓
fibrinogen level, fibrin degradation product, thrombocytopenia).

question: Consider the following statements:  Down's syndrome is characterized by:  1. Higher incidence of congenital heart disease  2. Hirschsprung's disease  3. Duodenal atresia Which of the statements given above are correct?
Which one of the  following antihypertensive drugs is contraindicated in pregnancy?
explanation:
Antihypertensives to be avoided during
pregnancy • Diuretics—by reducing blood volume, accentuate uteroplacental
perfusion deficit of toxemia—increased risk of fetal wastage, placental infarcts,
miscarriage, stillbirth. • Propranolol—causes LBW, decreases placental size,
neonatal bradycardia, and hypoglycemia. • Sodium nitroprusside—
contraindicated in eclampsia. • Reserpine—suicidal tendency in mother, nasal
obstruction, deranged respiratory, and temperature control in newborn. • ACE
inhibitors (enalapril, captopril) should be avoided in pregnancy, as they cause
fetal oligohydramnios, IUGR, fetal, or neonatal renal failure.

question: A pregnant woman with mitral stenosis with grade II dyspnea at 38  weeks comes to the antenatal clinic. The appropriate management in this  case would be

options.a: Induction of labor immediately to avoid further cardiac load
options.b: Outpatient management with tablets digoxin and Lasix till she goes in labor
options.c: Admission of the patient to achieve the maximum functional capacity of the  heart
options.d: Perform immediate cesarean section to avoid further cardiac load and  deterioration

explanation:
Admission of patient with heart disease in
pregnancy
1. Elective a. Grade I—at least 2 weeks prior to EDD. b. Grade II—at 28th
week, especially, in case of unfavorable social surroundings. c. Grades III
and IV—as soon as pregnancy is diagnosed and kept in hospital
throughout pregnancy.
2. Emergency a. Deterioration of functional grading. b. Appearance of
dyspnea or cough or basal crepitations or tachyarrhythmia. c.
Appearance of any pregnancy complication like anemia, pre-eclampsia.
Place of cesarean section: In general, there is no indication of cesarean
section for heart disease. Cesarean section is mainly done for obstetric
indications and in coarctation of aorta. Reference: Dutta, Textbook of
Obstetrics, 7th Ed., Pg. 278.

question: Which one of the following should not be done during the delivery of an  Rh -ve mother?

options.d: Forceps application in second stage of labor

explanation:
Care during delivery
1. Vaginal delivery: (i) Careful fetal monitoring, (ii) withheld prophylactic
ergometrine during 2nd stage, (iii) gentle handling of the uterus in 3rd
stage, (iv) to take care of postpartum hemorrhage.
2. Cesarean section: (i) To avoid spillage of blood into the peritoneal cavity,
(ii) routine manual removal of placenta should be withheld.
3. Clamping the umbilical cord: Cord is to be clamped as quickly as possible
to minimize even minute amount of Ab to cross the fetus. Cord should
be kept long (15–20 cm) for exchange transfusion, if required.
4. Collection of cord blood: 5 mL blood (2 mL oxalated and 3 mL clotted)
should be collected. Cord should not be squeezed to prevent
contamination with Wharton's jelly.
o Clotted blood: ABO and Rh grouping, direct Coombs test, and
serum bilirubin.
o Oxalated blood: Hemoglobin, blood smear administers 300 µg Rh
immune globin to the mother soon after birth if the baby is Rh
positive, and there is no evidence of maternal antibodies. Factor
protecting Rh-incompatibility: Nonresponder status of the mother
who fails to sensitize by Rh factor, ABO incompatibility, other less
understood mechanism. References: Dutta, Textbook of Obstetrics,
7th Ed., Pg. 335-7; Holland and Brews, 3rd Ed., Pg. 155.

question: Consider the following statements regarding labor:  1. Dilatation of cervix occurs at a rate of 1 cm per hour in active stage of  labor.  2. Latent phase of labor is up to 3 cm dilation of cervix.  3. There are 4 stages of labor.  4. Partogram is an excellent tool for the management of labor. Which of the  statements given above are correct?
An 18-year-old girl who has not attained menarche comes to casualty  with acute retention of urine. The most likely diagnosis is
explanation:
Imperforate hymen: The young girls
present with colicky abdominal pain which is often cyclic. Menarche has not yet
set in; however, secondary sexual characters have been well developed. As the
vagina gets progressively and increasingly distended with menstrual blood
(hematocolpos), pressure symptoms follow (difficulty during micturition finally
ends up with urinary retention). If the condition is neglected, vaginal distension
progressed beyond the hematocolpos to hematosalpinx and hematometra.
Reference: Shaw, 15th Ed., Pg. 96.

question: Congenital adrenal hyperplasia commonly results from the deficiency of  which one of the following enzymes?
The cyst which arises from the remnants of mesonephric duct and lies in  the anterolateral aspect of the vaginal wall is
explanation:
• Bartholin's cyst: Cystic swelling at junction of posterior one-third and
anterior two-thirds of labium majus. [Shaw—Bartholin's cyst lies
posterolaterally in relation to vaginal orifice]. It is a compound racemose
gland and acini are lined by low columnar epithelium (Mnemonic—BPL).
• Gartner's duct cyst: Remnant of Wolffian duct runs parallel and below
the fallopian tube in mesosalpinx—downward by side of uterus to the
level of internal os where it passes into the tissue of the cervix then
forward to reach the anterolateral aspect of vaginal wall and hymen
(Mnemonic—GAL).
• Mesonephric duct and tubules give rise to: (i) epoophoron—equivalent
to male ductus deferens and is also called Gartner's duct, (ii)
paroophoron. References: Harrison, 16th Ed., Pg. 858; Singh,
Embryology, 7th Ed., Pg. 299.

question: Which one of the following karyotypes is diagnostic of Mayer- Rokitansky-Kuster-Hauser syndrome?
explanation:
Three most common
causes of primary amenorrhea: (i) gonadal dysgenesis (30%)—Turner's
syndrome, (ii) Mullerian agenesis (20%) also known as Rokitansky-Kuster-
Hauser syndrome, and (iii) androgen insensitivity (10%).

question: What is the maternal cause of big baby born to a diabetic mother?
Breech presentation is common in the following conditions except
explanation:
Etiology of breech presentation
1. Maternal—multiparity, polyhydramnios, oligohydramnios, placenta
previa, pelvic tumors, congenital uterine abnormalities (septate,
bicornuate cornual implantation of placenta).
2. Fetal—prematurity (one of the most common), multiple pregnancy, fetal
abnormalities, e.g., hydrocephalus and anencephaly. Breech with
extended legs, short cord, relative or absolute, intrauterine fetal death.
Trisomies 13, 18, 21, and myotonic dystrophy. Uterus didelphys and
unicornuate favor breech presentation, whereas subseptate and
bicornuate favor transverse lie. Congenital uterine abnormalities, e.g.,
bicornuate and septate favors breech. References: Dutta, Textbook of
Obstetrics, 7th Ed., Pg. 375; Jaffcoats, Pg. 154; Daftary, Manual of
Obstetrics.

question: The number of stillbirths plus neonatal deaths per 1000 total births is  called as
explanation:
Perinatal mortality rate: Late
fetal and early neonatal deaths weighing more than 1000 g at birth expressed
as a ratio per 1000 live births weighing more than 1000 g at birth.
• Neonatal mortality rate is the number of neonatal deaths in a given year
per 1000 live births in that year.
• Infant mortality rate is the ratio of infant deaths registered in a given
year to the total number of live births registered in the same year,
usually expressed as a rate per 1000 live births. Causes of neonatal
mortality are:-
• The first 28 days of life - the neonatal period, is the most vulnerable time
for a child's survival.
• The neonatal mortality is directly related to the birth weight and
gestational age. Intrapartum related complications, low birth weight and
preterm birth is a causal factor in 60 % of neonate deaths. Prematurity
and congenital anomalies account for 60 %. 25% are attributable to
asphyxia mainly in first week. In the late neonatal period, i.e after the
first week, deaths attributable to infection (including diarrhoea and
tetanus) predominate. NMR of babies born to mothers with no
education are nearly twice as high as those of babies born to mothers
with secondary education or higher. The family's wealth and rural/urban
residence also remain powerful determinant of neonatal mortality.
Ending child marriage, reducing adolescent pregnancy and extending
birth intervals are reducing the risk of newborn mortality. NMR is also
linked to the health of the mother and the care she receives before,
during and immediately after giving birth. Nearly three quarters of all
neonatal deaths could be prevented if women were adequately
nourished and received appropriate care during pregnancy, childbirth
and in the postnatal period. Neonatal mortality is greater in boys
throughout the world, because newborn boys are biologically more
fragile than girls. References: Dutta, Textbook of Obstetrics, 7th Ed., Pg.
605; Park, 25th Ed., Pg. 619.

question: Umbilical cord insertion at the placental margin is called as
explanation:
• Velamentous placenta: Cord attached to the membranes. A velamentous
cord insertion may result in tearing of the vessels while exercising
controlled cord traction during 3rd stage of labor.
• Vasa previa: If the leash of blood vessels happens to traverse through the
membranes overlying the internal os, in front of presenting part, the
condition is called vasa previa. With formation of lower uterine segments
and dilation of internal os these vessels may rupture and start bleeding.
This blood is fetal blood.
• A battledore insertion of cord into the lower margin of the placenta may
predispose to a cord presentation or cord prolapse.
• Variations in placental form include lobed placenta, succenturiate lobe,
which is an accessory lobe at some distance from the main placental
mass and connected to it by membranes containing branches of
umbilical vessels. Reference: Dutta, Textbook of Obstetrics, 7th Ed., Pg.
218.

question: Which one of the following is contraindicated if lactation is to be  suppressed?

options.a: Expression of milk from engorged breasts

explanation:
Lactation
failure—(i) infrequent suckling, (ii) endogenous suppression of prolactin (ergot
preparations, pyridoxine, diuretics, retained placental bits). Lactation
suppression
• Drugs: Bromocriptine, 1.25–2.50 mg twice daily for 2 weeks; ethinyl
estradiol, 0.05 × 3 for 5 days; cabergoline, 0.5 mg 2 tab. stat.;
combination of testosterone and estrogen [Dutta—estrogen, androgen,
bromocryptine not recommended].
• Mechanical methods: (i) Stop breastfeeding; (ii) should not express or
pump out the milk from the breast; (iii) a tight compression bandage is
applied for about 2–3 days; (iv) analgesic tablets containing aspirin and
ice packs are given to relieve pain and breast engorgement.
• Drugs to improve milk production: Metoclopramide, sulpiride, intranasal
oxytocin. References: Dutta, Textbook of Obstetrics, 7th Ed., Pg. 149;
Holland and Brews, 3rd Ed., Pg. 381.

question: A grand multipara is referred with history of prolonged labor and  sudden sharp pain in abdomen and bleeding per vagina. On examination,  fetal heart sounds are absent and fetal parts are easily felt. The most likely  diagnosis is
explanation:
Rupture uterus
During pregnancy
During labor
• Scar rupture: Classical or
hysterotomy scar rupture—clinical
features—dull abdominal pain over
scar area with slight vaginal bleeding,
tenderness on palpation, FHS—
irregular or absent.• Lower segment
scar rupture: Onset insidious, no
• Scar rupture: Classical or hysterotomy
scar rupture—features same as those
occur during pregnancy.• Spontaneous
obstructive rupture: Initially pain is
severe, gradually becomes continuous
and confined to suprapubic region,
tender, Bandl's ring may be visible, and
classical features, called "silent
rupture."• Spontaneous rupture in
uninjured uterus: Confined to high
parous women. Onset—usually acute
with acute pain in abdomen with
fainting attacks and may collapse.
Diagnosis by presence of features of
shock, acute tenderness on
abdominal examination, and
palpation of superficial fetal parts, if
the rupture is complete and absence
of fetal heart rate. However, with
insidious onset, the diagnosis is often
confused with concealed accidental
hemorrhage or rectus sheath
hematoma.• Rupture following fall,
blow, or external version or use of
oxytocics: History of an accident
followed acute pain abdomen and
slight vaginal bleeding.
on rupture dull aching pain. On
examination, superficial fetal parts
reveal, absence of FHS, absence of
uterine contour, two separate swellings
(one contracted uterus and other fetal
ovoid).• Spontaneous nonobstructive
rupture confined to high parous
women.• Rupture following
manipulative or instrumental delivery.
Reference: Dutta, Textbook of Obstetrics, 7th Ed., Pg. 428–30.

question: A woman presents with preterm rupture of membranes with vertex  presentation and gestational age of greater than 34 weeks; the appropriate  management is

options.b: To wait for spontaneous labor for 6 hours
options.c: To wait for spontaneous labor for 24 hours

explanation:
Prelabor
rupture of membranes (PROM)—any time beyond 28th week of pregnancy but
before the onset of labor. Term prelabor rupture of the membranes—when
rupture occurs beyond 37 weeks but before onset of labor. Preterm prelabor
rupture of the membranes occurs before 37 completed weeks. Prolonged
rupture of membranes—rupture of membrane for >24 hours before delivery.
Flowchart for management of PROM—once diagnosis is confirmed,
management depends on: (i) gestational age of fetus, (ii) whether the patient is
in labor or not, (iii) any evidence of sepsis, (iv) prospect of fetal survival in that
institution, if delivery occurs, (v) maternal pulse, temperature, and fetal heart
rate monitored 4 hourly, (vi) prophylactic antibiotics (IV ampicillin and
erythromycin for 48 hours followed by oral therapy for 5 days). Reference:
Dutta, Textbook of Obstetrics, 7th Ed., Pg. 318.

question: A patient has been referred with prolonged second stage of labor. On  examination, there is severe molding of the head and the station of head is  +1. Fetal heart rate is 120/minute, and she is having good uterine  contractions. Which one of the following should be done?

Match list I (procedure) with list II (management of obstetric  complications) and select the correct answer using the codes given below the  lists: List I (Procedure) A. Burn Marshall technique B. Lovsett maneuver C.  Brandt-Andrews technique D. Pinard's maneuver  List II (Management of obstetric complications)  1. Delivery of the placenta by controlled cord traction  2. To deliver extended leg in breech  3. To deliver after coming head in breech  4. To deliver extended arms in breech
The following are indications for lower segment cesarean section  except

options.a: Fetal distress in first stage of labor
options.c: Cancer cervix diagnosed at 38 weeks of pregnancy

explanation:
Indications of present day classical cesarean section
1. Lower segment cannot be easily approached due to dense adhesions,
presence of fibroids, or marked contraction of the pelvis in
kyphoscoliotic pelvis.
2. The lower uterine segment is highly vascular due to the underlying
placenta previa.
3. The lower segment is infiltrated with advanced cancer of the cervix.
After delivery of the baby, a Wertheim's operation may be performed.
4. In occasional cases of obstructed labor due to transverse lie and an
impacted shoulder presentation.
5. Threatened rupture or dehiscence or previous classical scar.
6. Rare cases of conjoined twins. Reference: Dutta, Textbook of Obstetrics,
7th Ed. Pg. 590.

explanation:

medical complications (diabetes
mellitus, chronic renal disease)•
Postmaturity• Abruptio placenta•
IUGR• Rh isoimmunization•
Premature rupture of membranes•
Fetus with a major congenital

question: For recent fecal pollution of water, confirmatory evidence is provided  by the presence of
explanation:
The primary bacterial indicator of fecal
pollution—coliform (typical example of fecal group of E. coli and of the
nonfecal group of Klebsiella aerogenes).
• For recent fecal pollution—fecal streptococci.
• For remote contamination—Clostridium perfringens (in absence of
coliform). Reference: Park, 23rd Ed., Pg. 721.

question: Which one of the following properties best describes fatal poisoning  caused by carbon monoxide?

options.a: It has a cellular toxin that causes tracheobronchitis
options.c: It interferes with hemoglobin's ability to carry oxygen

explanation:
Carbon monoxide poisoning
• Source—fire smoke, automobile exhaust, fumes from faulty gas stoves,
and ingestion of paint and varnish removers.
• Mechanism—binds to Hb with an affinity 210 times that of oxygen. This
binding:
1. Reduces oxygen transport by Hb.
2. Decreases the release of oxygen to tissue (oxygen dissociation
curve shifts to left).
3. Binds to myoglobin, decreasing its oxygen-carrying capacity.
4. Binds to mitochondrial cytochrome oxidase, inhibiting cellular
respiration. The net effect is profound tissue hypoxia, anaerobic
metabolism, and lactic acidosis.
• Toxicity
1. Mild symptoms—15–20% carboxyhemoglobin fraction
2. Moderate symptoms—20–40% carboxyhemoglobin fraction
3. Severe symptoms—40–50% carboxyhemoglobin fraction
4. Fatal symptoms—>60% carboxyhemoglobin fraction
• Clinical manifestations—headache, shortness of breath, dyspnea,
tachypnea, cyanosis, convulsion, coma. In comatose patients, blisters
and bullae may develop over pressure points. A cherry red color of skin
and mucous membrane are rare.
• Treatment with 100% oxygen and hyperbaric oxygen, if
carboxyhemoglobin level is above 40%. Reference: Ghai, 6th Ed., Pg. 645.

question: Amino acid score as an indicator of quality of protein refers to which  one of the following:

options.a: It is a product of digestibility coefficient and biological value
options.c: Number of milligrams of one amino acid per gram of protein
options.d: Measure of the concentration of each amino acid in the test protein  expressed as percentage of that amino acid in the reference protein

explanation:
Protein quality assessed by comparison with the "reference
protein" which is usually an egg protein. Methods of assessment of protein
quality:
1. Amino acid score is a measure of the concentration of each essential
amino acid in the test protein expressed as a percentage of that amino
acid in the reference protein. Amino acid score = (No. of milligrams of
one amino acid per gram of protein / No. of milligrams of the same
amino acid per gram of protein) × 100 This is 50–60 for starch and 70 and
80 for amino foods.
2. Net protein utilization (NPU)—product of digestibility coefficient and
biological value divided by 100. NPU = (Nitrogen retained by body /
Nitrogen intake) × 100 NPU gives more complete expression of protein
quality than amino acid score. In calculating protein quality, 1 g of
protein is assumed to be equivalent to 6.25 g of N. The NPU of the
protein of Indian diets varies between 50 and 80. The ICMR expert group
suggested an intake of 1 g of protein per kilogram of body weight for
adult male and female assuming an NPU of 65. Reference: Park, 23rd Ed.,
Pg. 634.
